title: GR0: Graph Fundamentals
text:
GR0: Graph Fundamentals 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 3 (Decompositions of Graphs) This is the foundation for everything. If you struggled with graphs before, this is where we fix that. 1. What Is a Graph? A graph G = (V, E) consists of: V — a set of vertices (aka nodes) E — a set of edges connecting pairs of vertices graph LR A --- B A --- C B --- C Two flavors: Undirected: edges are unordered pairs {u, v}. If A connects to B, then B connects to A. Directed: edges are ordered pairs (u, v). A → B does NOT mean B → A. graph LR A -->|directed| B A -->|directed| C B -->|directed| C We assume all graphs are simple — no self-loops (u, u) and no parallel edges. 2. Terminology Term Definition Adjacent Two vertices sharing an edge Neighborhood N(v) Set of vertices adjacent to v Degree deg(v) Number of edges incident to v; equals |N(v)| In-degree (directed) Number of edges coming into v Out-degree (directed) Number of edges going out of v Path Sequence of vertices connected by edges Cycle Path that starts and ends at the same vertex Connected (undirected) There's a path between every pair of vertices Strongly connected (directed) For every u, v: path from u→v AND v→u DAG Directed Acyclic Graph — directed graph with no cycles Source Vertex with in-degree 0 (nothing points to it) Sink Vertex with out-degree 0 (it points to nothing) ⚠️ Common Pitfall: In directed graphs, adjacency has direction. "A is adjacent to B" means there's an edge A → B. It does NOT mean B → A. 3. Graph Representations Adjacency Matrix An n × n matrix M where M[i][j] = 1 if edge (i, j) exists, 0 otherwise. Example (undirected): Example (directed): 1 2 3 1 2 3 1 [ 0 1 1 ] 1 [ 0 1 1 ] 2 [ 1 0 1 ] 2 [ 0 0 1 ] 3 [ 1 1 0 ] 3 [ 0 1 0 ] Space: O(n²) Check if edge exists: O(1) ✅ List all neighbors: O(n) ❌ Undirected → symmetric matrix Adjacency List Each vertex stores a list of its neighbors. Example (directed): 1 → [2, 3] 2 → [3] 3 → [2] Space: O(n + m) where m = |E| Check if edge exists: O(degree) ❌ List all neighbors: O(degree) ✅ 💡 Intuition: For sparse graphs (m ≪ n²), adjacency lists win. Most real-world graphs are sparse. This course uses adjacency lists unless stated otherwise. Adjacency Matrix Adjacency List Space O(n²) O(n + m) Edge lookup O(1) O(degree) Iterate neighbors O(n) O(degree) Best for Dense graphs Sparse graphs (most cases) 4. BFS: Breadth-First Search BFS explores the graph level by level, like ripples in a pond. It finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs. BFS(G, s): Input: G = (V, E), source vertex s Output: dist[v] = min edges from s to v prev[v] = predecessor on shortest path for all v ∈ V: dist[v] = ∞ prev[v] = null dist[s] = 0 Q = queue containing s while Q is not empty: v = dequeue(Q) for all edges (v, w) ∈ E: if dist[w] = ∞: // not yet visited dist[w] = dist[v] + 1 prev[w] = v enqueue(Q, w) Runtime: O(n + m) — each vertex and edge visited once. BFS Example graph LR A((A
d=0)) --> B((B
d=1)) A --> C((C
d=1)) B --> D((D
d=2)) C --> D C --> E((E
d=2)) Starting from A: BFS visits {A} → {B, C} → {D, E} 🎯 Exam Tip: BFS gives shortest paths in unweighted graphs only. For weighted graphs, you need Dijkstra's. 5. Dijkstra's Algorithm Dijkstra's is BFS for weighted graphs (with positive weights only). Dijkstra(G, s, w): Input: G = (V, E), source s, weights w(e) > 0 for all e Output: dist[v] = shortest weighted path from s to v prev[v] = predecessor on shortest path for all v ∈ V: dist[v] = ∞ prev[v] = null dist[s] = 0 H = min-heap with all vertices, keyed by dist while H is not empty: v = extractMin(H) for all edges (v, w) ∈ E: if dist[v] + w(v,w) < dist[w]: dist[w] = dist[v] + w(v,w) prev[w] = v decreaseKey(H, w) Runtime: O((|V| + |E|) log |V|) using a binary min-heap. Key lemmas: The subpath of any shortest path is itself a shortest path Triangle inequality: dist(u, v) ≤ dist(u, x) + dist(x, v) ⚠️ Common Pitfall: Dijkstra's does NOT work with negative edge weights. For negative weights, use Bellman-Ford (covered in DP3). 6. DFS: Depth-First Search (Undirected) DFS explores as deep as possible before backtracking. It's the foundation for SCCs, topological sort, and cycle detection. DFS for Finding Connected Components DFS(G): Input: G = (V, E) undirected, adjacency list Output: ccnum[v] = connected component number for each v cc = 0 for all v ∈ V: visited[v] = false for all v ∈ V: if not visited[v]: cc = cc + 1 Explore(v) Explore(z): visited[z] = true ccnum[z] = cc for all (z, w) ∈ E: if not visited[w]: Explore(w) Runtime: O(n + m) 💡 Intuition: Each call to Explore from the main DFS loop discovers a new connected component. Everything reachable from z gets the same cc number. graph TD subgraph "Component 1" A --- B B --- C end subgraph "Component 2" D --- E end subgraph "Component 3" F end 7. DFS for Directed Graphs — Pre/Post Numbering For directed graphs, we add a clock to track the order of discovery (pre) and completion (post) for each vertex. DFS(G): Input: G = (V, E) directed, adjacency list Output: pre[v], post[v] for each vertex clock = 1 for all v ∈ V: visited[v] = false for all v ∈ V: if not visited[v]: Explore(v) Explore(z): pre[z] = clock; clock++ visited[z] = true for all (z, w) ∈ E: if not visited[w]: Explore(w) post[z] = clock; clock++ Runtime: O(n + m) Worked Example graph LR A --> B A --> C B --> D B --> E D --> E E --> A Running DFS starting from A: Vertex pre post A 1 10 B 2 9 D 3 6 E 4 5 C 7 8 The pre/post intervals tell us about the tree structure: Containment: If [pre(u), post(u)] contains [pre(v), post(v)], then u is an ancestor of v Disjoint: If intervals don't overlap, neither is an ancestor of the other 8. Edge Types in DFS When we run DFS on a directed graph, every edge falls into exactly one of four categories: Edge Type Relationship Post Numbers Tree edge Parent → child in DFS tree post(z) > post(w) Back edge Descendant → ancestor post(z) < post(w) ⚠️ Forward edge Ancestor → non-child descendant post(z) > post(w) Cross edge Between unrelated vertices post(z) > post(w) graph TD A -->|tree| B A -->|tree| C B -->|tree| D D -->|back| A A -->|forward| D C -->|cross| D ⚠️ Common Pitfall: Back edges are the ONLY type where post(z) < post(w). For all other edges, post(z) > post(w). This is critical for understanding cycles and topological sort. The Cycle Theorem G has a cycle ⟺ its DFS tree has a back edge. Proof (→): Consider a cycle C. Let v_i be the first vertex of C visited by DFS. All other cycle vertices are reachable from v_i, so they become descendants. The edge v_{i-1} → v_i goes from descendant to ancestor = back edge. ✅ Proof (←): A back edge v → w means v is a descendant of w. So there's a path w ↝ v in the tree, plus the edge v → w. That's a cycle. ✅ 9. Topological Sorting A topological ordering of a DAG is a linear ordering of vertices such that for every edge u → v, u comes before v. 💡 Intuition: Think of it as a valid scheduling order. If task A must happen before task B, then A appears first. The Algorithm: Run DFS, then sort vertices by dec

title: GR1: Strongly Connected Components
text: GR1: Strongly Connected Components 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 3.4 SCCs are the directed-graph analog of connected components. The algorithm is elegant: just two passes of DFS. 1. Definition Strongly Connected: Vertices v and w are strongly connected if there's a path v ↝ w AND a path w ↝ v. Strongly Connected Component (SCC): A maximal set of mutually strongly connected vertices. "Maximal" means you can't add any more vertices and still have everyone strongly connected. graph LR subgraph SCC1["SCC: A,B,E"] A --> B B --> E E --> A end subgraph SCC2["SCC: C,D"] C --> D D --> C end subgraph SCC3["SCC: F"] F end B --> C D --> F E --> F This graph has 3 SCCs: {A, B, E}, {C, D}, and {F}. 2. The Metagraph — Every Directed Graph Is a DAG of Its SCCs If you collapse each SCC into a single "meta-vertex" and keep the edges between them, you get a DAG. graph LR S1["SCC: A,B,E"] --> S2["SCC: C,D"] S1 --> S3["SCC: F"] S2 --> S3 Key Property: The metagraph of SCCs is always a DAG. Why? If there were a cycle between two SCCs S and S', then every vertex in S could reach every vertex in S' and vice versa — meaning they should be merged into one bigger SCC. Contradiction. 3. The Goal We want to find all SCCs of a directed graph in O(n + m) time. Strategy: Think of the metagraph as a DAG. If we can identify the SCCs one at a time (say, starting from sinks), we can peel them off. Key insight: If we pick any vertex v in a sink SCC and run Explore(v), we visit exactly the vertices in that SCC (and nothing else — because a sink SCC has no outgoing edges in the metagraph). So the plan is: Find a vertex in a sink SCC Explore from it → discover the entire sink SCC Remove it Repeat 4. Finding a Sink SCC via the Reverse Graph Problem: How do we find a vertex guaranteed to be in a sink SCC? 💡 Intuition: We can't directly find a sink SCC, but we CAN find a source SCC — it's the one containing the vertex with the highest post number after DFS. And here's the trick: sources in G^R are sinks in G (and vice versa). G^R is the reverse graph — same vertices, all edges flipped: E^R = {(w, v) : (v, w) ∈ E} The SCCs are the same in G and G^R (reversing edges doesn't change strong connectivity). But sources and sinks swap! The Key Lemma Lemma: The vertex with the highest post number (from DFS on G^R) lies in a source SCC of G^R, which is a sink SCC of G. Proof sketch: For SCCs S and S' with an edge S → S' in the metagraph, the maximum post number in S is greater than the max post number in S'. The SCC with the globally highest post number must therefore be a source (nothing has a higher post number pointing into it). In G^R, this source corresponds to a sink in G. 5. The SCC Algorithm SCC(G): Input: Directed graph G = (V, E) Output: ccnum[v] = SCC number for each vertex 1. Construct G^R (reverse all edges) 2. Run DFS(G^R) — compute post numbers 3. Order vertices by decreasing post number 4. Run DFS(G) using this vertex ordering — each call to Explore from the main loop discovers a new SCC Runtime: O(n + m) Step 1: O(n + m) to construct reverse graph Step 2: O(n + m) for DFS Step 3: O(n) for ordering (or just process in decreasing order) Step 4: O(n + m) for DFS Why Step 4 Works In Step 4, we process vertices in decreasing post-number order from G^R. The first vertex we pick is in a sink SCC of G. Explore visits exactly that SCC (it can't escape — no outgoing metagraph edges). We mark it and continue. The next unvisited vertex with the highest remaining post number is in the next sink SCC (after removing the previous one). And so on. 6. Full Worked Example Consider this directed graph G: graph LR A --> B B --> C C --> A B --> D D --> E E --> D C --> E Step 1: Construct G^R (reverse all edges) graph LR B --> A C --> B A --> C D --> B E --> D D --> E E --> C Step 2: Run DFS on G^R (starting from, say, A) Vertex pre post A 1 10 C 2 9 B 3 8 D 4 7 E 5 6 Step 3: Order by decreasing post number A (10), C (9), B (8), D (7), E (6) Step 4: Run DFS on G (original) in this order Start with A → Explore finds: A → B → C → (A already visited) → D → E → (D already visited). Wait — this visits everything! Hmm, let me redo this more carefully. Let's use a different example where SCCs are clearer. Better Example graph LR A --> B B --> A B --> C C --> D D --> C D --> E SCCs: {A, B}, {C, D}, {E} Step 1: G^R: graph LR B --> A A --> B C --> B D --> C C --> D E --> D Step 2: DFS on G^R (start from A): Explore A → B → (A visited) → C → D → (C visited) → E → done backtracking. Vertex pre post A 1 10 B 2 9 C 3 8 D 4 7 E 5 6 Step 3: Order by decreasing post: A, B, C, D, E Step 4: DFS on G in this order: Explore(A): visits A → B → (A visited) → C → D → (C visited) → E Oops, this is because {A,B} can reach {C,D} can reach {E} OK — the trick is that the DFS in step 4 uses the undirected-style DFS (with component labeling). Each Explore call from the outer loop labels a new SCC. But within Explore, we only follow edges in G. Actually, let me reconsider. The key is that Step 4's DFS processes vertices in the order from Step 3. Since A has the highest post number in G^R, it's in a source SCC of G^R = sink SCC of G. But {A,B} is NOT a sink SCC here... Let me recheck. In G: {A,B} → {C,D} → {E}. So {E} is the sink SCC, {A,B} is the source. In G^R: edges are reversed, so {E} → {C,D} → {A,B}. So {A,B} is the sink SCC of G^R = source SCC of G^R... wait. The algorithm says: run DFS on G^R to find vertex ordering, then run DFS on G. The vertex with the highest post in G^R is in a source SCC of G^R = source SCC of G^R. But we want to find sink SCCs of G = source SCCs of G^R. ✅ So in G^R, vertex A has post=10 (highest). A is in a source SCC of G^R. Source of G^R = Sink of G... but {A,B} is NOT the sink of G! Let me reconsider: In G^R: {A,B} has an incoming edge from C (C→B in G^R). So {A,B} is NOT a source in G^R. {E} has an outgoing edge E→D in G^R, and incoming? Nothing points to E in G^R. So {E} is a source in G^R. But E has post=6, the LOWEST. I think the issue is my DFS traversal on G^R. Let me redo it starting from E: DFS on G^R starting from E: Explore(E): E → D → C → B → A → (B visited, back to A) → (back up) ... Starting vertex choice matters! DFS iterates over ALL vertices. Let's say the outer loop goes A, B, C, D, E: Explore(A): A → B (in G^R, edges from A are: A→C via C→A reversed? No: G has A→B, B→A, B→C, C→D, D→C, D→E. So G^R has: B→A, A→B, C→B, D→C, C→D, E→D. G^R adjacency list: A: [B] (from B→A in G^R; because A→B in G) B: [A] (from A→B in G^R; because B→A in G) C: [B, D] (from B→C reversed gives C→B; D→C reversed gives C→D) D: [C, E] (from C→D reversed gives D→C; but wait... C→D in G means D→C in G^R. And D→E in G means E→D in G^R) Let me redo: G edges: A→B, B→A, B→C, C→D, D→C, D→E G^R edges: B→A, A→B, C→B, D→C, C→D, E→D G^R adjacency list: A: [B] B: [A] C: [B, D] D: [C] E: [D] DFS on G^R, outer loop order A, B, C, D, E: Explore(A): visit A, go to B. Visit B, go to A (visited). Backtrack. post(B)=2, post(A)=3. B already visited. Skip. Ex

title: GR2: 2-Satisfiability (2-SAT)
text: GR2: 2-Satisfiability (2-SAT) 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 3.4 (Applications of SCC) This is a beautiful application of SCCs. We reduce a logic problem to a graph problem and solve it in linear time. 1. SAT and CNF — The Setup Boolean Formulas We work with boolean variables x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ, each either TRUE (1) or FALSE (0). For each variable xᵢ, we have two literals: xᵢ and x̄ᵢ (its negation/complement). Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) A formula in CNF is an AND of OR-clauses: f = (x2) ∧ (x̄3 ∨ x4) ∧ (x3 ∨ x̄5 ∨ x̄1 ∨ x2) Clause: An OR (∨) of literals, e.g., (x₃ ∨ x̄₅ ∨ x̄₁) Formula: An AND (∧) of clauses Satisfying assignment: Values for each variable that make the entire formula TRUE This means at least one literal in every clause must be TRUE The SAT Problem Input: A formula f in CNF with n variables and m clauses Output: A satisfying assignment, or "NO" if none exists 2. k-SAT k-SAT restricts every clause to have at most k literals. k Complexity 1 Trivial (each clause forces a variable) 2 Polynomial — O(n + m) via SCCs! 3+ NP-complete (no known polynomial algorithm) The jump from k=2 to k=3 is one of the most important complexity boundaries in CS. We're going to solve 2-SAT. 3. The Implication Graph Here's the key idea: every 2-SAT clause can be rewritten as two implications. For a clause (α ∨ β): If α is FALSE, then β MUST be TRUE → implication: ᾱ → β If β is FALSE, then α MUST be TRUE → implication: β̄ → α Construction Given a 2-SAT formula with n variables and m clauses: Vertices: 2n vertices — one for each literal xᵢ and x̄ᵢ Edges: 2m directed edges — two implications per clause Clause Edge 1 Edge 2 (α ∨ β) ᾱ → β β̄ → α Example f = (x̄1 ∨ x̄2) ∧ (x2 ∨ x3) ∧ (x̄3 ∨ x̄1) Clause Implications Edges (x̄₁ ∨ x̄₂) x₁ → x̄₂, x₂ → x̄₁ x₁ → x̄₂, x₂ → x̄₁ (x₂ ∨ x₃) x̄₂ → x₃, x̄₃ → x₂ x̄₂ → x₃, x̄₃ → x₂ (x̄₃ ∨ x̄₁) x₃ → x̄₁, x₁ → x̄₃ x₃ → x̄₁, x₁ → x̄₃ graph LR x1["x1"] --> nx2["not-x2"] x2["x2"] --> nx1["not-x1"] nx2 --> x3["x3"] nx3["not-x3"] --> x2 x3 --> nx1 x1 --> nx3 💡 Intuition: Paths in this graph are chains of logical implications. If there's a path x₁ ↝ x̄₁, it means "if x₁ is TRUE, then x₁ must be FALSE" — a contradiction (unless x₁ is just FALSE). 4. The Key Lemma Lemma: A 2-SAT formula f is satisfiable if and only if for all i, xᵢ and x̄ᵢ are in different SCCs of the implication graph. Equivalently: f is unsatisfiable ⟺ there exists some i where xᵢ and x̄ᵢ are in the same SCC. Why "same SCC = unsatisfiable" If xᵢ and x̄ᵢ are in the same SCC, then: There's a path xᵢ ↝ x̄ᵢ (if xᵢ is TRUE, then x̄ᵢ must be TRUE → contradiction) There's a path x̄ᵢ ↝ xᵢ (if x̄ᵢ is TRUE, then xᵢ must be TRUE → same contradiction) Neither assignment works → unsatisfiable Why "different SCCs = satisfiable" This is the harder direction. We need to actually construct a satisfying assignment. That's where the algorithm comes in. 5. The 2-SAT Algorithm 2SAT(f): Input: Formula f in 2-CNF with n variables, m clauses Output: Satisfying assignment or "unsatisfiable" 1. Construct the implication graph G (2n vertices, 2m edges) 2. Run SCC algorithm on G → get SCCs in reverse topological order (sink first) 3. For each i: if xᵢ and x̄ᵢ are in the same SCC, return "unsatisfiable" 4. For each SCC S (in reverse topological order, sink → source): - If literals in S are unassigned: - Set all literals in S to TRUE - Set all complement literals (in S̄) to FALSE 5. Return assignment Runtime: O(n + m) — dominated by the SCC computation. 6. Why the Algorithm Is Correct The Assignment Strategy 💡 Intuition: Sink SCCs are "safe" to set TRUE because they have no outgoing implications to worry about. Their complements are source SCCs (safe to set FALSE because FALSE can imply anything). Claim: If S is a sink SCC, then S̄ is a source SCC Proof: First, we need a helper fact: Fact: If there's a path α ↝ β in the implication graph, then there's a path β̄ ↝ ᾱ. Why? Each edge ᾱ → β in the implication graph came from a clause (α ∨ β), which also produced the edge β̄ → ᾱ. Chaining these along a path gives us the complement path in reverse. Now, suppose S is a sink SCC. For any α ∈ S: There's no edge from α to any vertex outside S (sink property) By the helper fact, there's no edge from any vertex outside S̄ to ᾱ So S̄ has no incoming edges from outside → S̄ is a source SCC ✅ Why Setting Sink = TRUE Works When we set a sink SCC S to TRUE: S has no outgoing edges → no implications to propagate S̄ is set to FALSE. S̄ is a source → all edges from S̄ go outward. But FALSE can imply anything (FALSE → anything is vacuously TRUE), so no contradictions arise. After removing S and S̄, the remaining graph still has valid SCCs, and we repeat. 7. Worked Example f = (x1 ∨ x2) ∧ (x̄1 ∨ x3) ∧ (x̄2 ∨ x̄3) ∧ (x1 ∨ x3) Step 1: Build implication graph Clause Edge 1 Edge 2 (x₁ ∨ x₂) x̄₁ → x₂ x̄₂ → x₁ (x̄₁ ∨ x₃) x₁ → x₃ x̄₃ → x̄₁ (x̄₂ ∨ x̄₃) x₂ → x̄₃ x₃ → x̄₂ (x₁ ∨ x₃) x̄₁ → x₃ x̄₃ → x₁ graph LR nx1["not-x1"] --> x2["x2"] nx2["not-x2"] --> x1["x1"] x1 --> x3["x3"] nx3["not-x3"] --> nx1 x2 --> nx3 x3 --> nx2 nx1 --> x3 nx3 --> x1 Step 2: Find SCCs Let's trace the paths: x̄₁ → x₂ → x̄₃ → x₁ → x₃ → x̄₂ → x₁ (cycle: x₁, x₃, x̄₂, x̄₃ form paths but let's check) x̄₃ → x̄₁ → x₃ (via x̄₁ → x₃) — and x₃ → x̄₂ → x₁ → x₃ (cycle!) SCC analysis: x₁ → x₃ → x̄₂ → x₁: SCC A = {x₁, x₃, x̄₂} x̄₁ → x₂ → x̄₃ → x̄₁: SCC B = {x̄₁, x₂, x̄₃} Note: SCC A and SCC B are complements of each other! ✅ Step 3: Check — no xᵢ and x̄ᵢ in the same SCC. ✅ Satisfiable! Step 4: Assign SCC A = {x₁, x₃, x̄₂} and SCC B = {x̄₁, x₂, x̄₃} Which is the sink? A has edges: x₁ → x₃ (internal), x₃ → x̄₂ (internal), x̄₂ → x₁ (internal). Any outgoing? Check all edges from members of A to non-members... If A is the sink: Set SCC A = TRUE: x₁ = T, x₃ = T, x̄₂ = T → x₂ = F Set SCC B = FALSE: x̄₁ = F, x₂ = F, x̄₃ = F → x₁ = T, x₃ = T ✅ (consistent!) Verify: x₁ = T, x₂ = F, x₃ = T (x₁ ∨ x₂) = (T ∨ F) = T ✅ (x̄₁ ∨ x₃) = (F ∨ T) = T ✅ (x̄₂ ∨ x̄₃) = (T ∨ F) = T ✅ (x₁ ∨ x₃) = (T ∨ T) = T ✅ 8. Unit Clause Simplification (Preprocessing) If a clause has only one literal (a unit clause), we can simplify: Find unit clause with literal α Set α = TRUE Remove all clauses containing α Remove ᾱ from all remaining clauses (may create new unit clauses or empty clauses) If any clause becomes empty → unsatisfiable Repeat This preprocessing reduces the problem before building the implication graph. After removing all unit clauses, every clause has exactly 2 literals. 9. Summary Problem Given 2-CNF formula, find satisfying assignment Key idea Build implication graph, run SCC Satisfiable iff No xᵢ and x̄ᵢ in same SCC Assignment Sink SCC → TRUE, its complement (source) → FALSE Runtime O(n + m) Contrast 3-SAT is NP-complete! 🎯 Exam Tip: The construction of the implication graph is the critical step. Remember: clause (α ∨ β) gives edges ᾱ → β and β̄ → α. Each clause contributes exactly 2 edges. ⚠️ Common Pitfall: Don't confuse the implication graph edges. For (α ∨ β), the implication is "if NOT α, then β" — so the edge goes

Intro to Graduate Algorithms¶ Intro Basic Details Georgia Tech: Spring 2023 Instructor: Eric Vigoda Website: https://omscs.gatech.edu/cs-6515-intro-graduate-algorithms Videos: https://omscs.gatech.edu/cs-6515-graduate-algorithms-course-videos Old Course Webpage (Use at your own risk - looks pretty good to me) https://gtalgorithms.wordpress.com/ Old course on youtube, Looks awesome!! https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLAwxTw4SYaPkbWSEj_1iO7rILlWDJImW4 Textbooks Official Algorithms by Dasgupta, C. H. Papadimitriou, and U. V. Vazirani http://algorithmics.lsi.upc.edu/docs/Dasgupta-Papadimitriou-Vazirani.pdf Secondary Algorithm Design by J. Kleinberg and E. Tardos https://ict.iitk.ac.in/wp-content/uploads/CS345-Algorithms-II-Algorithm-Design-by-Jon-Kleinberg-Eva-Tardos.pdf Lecture/Videos https://omscs.gatech.edu/cs-6515-graduate-algorithms-course-videos Topics Covered DP: Dynamic Programming RA: Randomized Algorithms DC: Divide and Conquer GR: Graph Algorithm MF: MaxFlow Problems LP: Linear Programming NP: Nondeterministic Polynomial time Problems Other Resources MIT: https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-006-introduction-to-algorithms-fall-2011/resources/lecture-videos/ UWashington: https://courses.cs.washington.edu/courses/cse417/ UIllinois: https://mfleck.cs.illinois.edu/building-blocks/index-sp2020.html UIllinois: https://jeffe.cs.illinois.edu/teaching/algorithms/#notes Schedule W01 - Dynamic Programming (Lectures: DP1) W02 - Dynamic Programming (Lectures: DP2, DP3) W03 - Divide and Conquer I (Lectures: DC1, DC3, DC4) W04 - Divide and Conquer II (Lectures: DC5, DC2) Exam 1 (W01-W04) W05 - Modular Arithmetic and RSA (Lectures: RA1, RA2) W06 - Graph Algorithm I (Lectures: GR1,GR2) W07 - Graph Algorithm II and Max Flow I (Lectures: GR3,MF1) W08 - Max Flow II (Lectures: MF2,MF3,MF5) W09 - Max Flow III (Lectures: MF4) Exam 2 (W05-W09) W10 - Np Completeness (Lectures: NP1,NP2,NP3) W11 - Spring Break W12 - Linear Programming (Lectures: LP1,LP2,LP3) W13 - NP and LP (Lectures: LP4 & NP4) W14 - More Complexity (Lectures: NP5) Exam 3 (W10-W13) W15 - Markov Chains W16 - Final Week Final Exam - Cumulative - Optional Grading 14% Homework 7% Coding Projects 7% Mini-Quizzes 3% Logistic Quizzes 69% Exams 23% a piece A [85-100%] B [70-85%] C [50-70%] Refresher Pseudo Code There doesn't appear to be a definitive guide to pseudo code. In the loosest sense of the phrase it is somewhere between mathematical notation and code. The goal is to describe an algorithm such that any programmer can understand it, and implement it, without needing to know a particular programming language. Of course what this means is that algorithm written using constructs found only in some programming language would automatically be dis-qualified. ie range(0,5) is found in python and should not be used. There are some exceptions to this, sum/min/max are so prevalent that there existence is virtually guaranteed. Thus they are allowed in this class. Another interesting tip to use the word as a function. length(A) would be allowed as it's meaning can be inferred, while A.len() would not be allowed. Dot Notation What is also not allowed is any form of dot notation. ie T.max(), S.sum() etc etc. This is unique to OOP style and is not found in many languages. But max(T), max(T[⋅]) are both allowed. Scoping Is done using indentation, not using {} Array Indexing can be done using either () or [] however array indexing like A[3:5] is not allowed nor are little tricks like A[-1] Checking Allowed: equality(==,=) and inequality(!=,<>) BAD Examples Good examples # This is basically python for i in range(1,n+1): for i = 1 to n t[i] += 1 t[i] = t[i] + 1 # This looks like C/C++ for( i=1; i<=n; i++ ){ for i = 1 -> n t[i]++ ; t[i] = t[i] + 1 } for i = 1...n t[i] = t[i] + 1 # -1 indexing is # not language agnostic # returns the last element return T[-1] return T[n] # dot notation not allowed # simple functions allowed x = A.size() x = length(A) y = x.len() Each algorithm is graded on four parts: a. Def

title: 📝 Graphs Cheat Sheet — CS 6515
text: 📝 Graphs Cheat Sheet — CS 6515 Algorithms at a Glance Algorithm Input Output Runtime Key Idea BFS G, source s dist[], prev[] O(n+m) Queue; shortest paths (unweighted) Dijkstra's G, s, w(e)>0 dist[], prev[] O((V+E)log V) Min-heap BFS; positive weights only DFS G pre[], post[], ccnum[] O(n+m) Recursion; foundation for everything Topo Sort DAG ordered vertices O(n+m) DFS, sort by decreasing post number SCC Directed G SCC labels O(n+m) DFS on G^R, then DFS on G 2-SAT 2-CNF formula assignment or NO O(n+m) Implication graph + SCC Kruskal's Undirected G, weights MST O(E log V) Sort edges; Union-Find Prim's Undirected G, weights MST O(E log V) Grow tree; min-heap Key Lemmas & Theorems Cycle Detection G has a cycle ⟺ DFS tree has a back edge SCC Source Lemma Vertex with highest post number (in DFS on G^R) is in a source SCC of G^R = sink SCC of G 2-SAT Lemma f is satisfiable ⟺ ∀i, xᵢ and x̄ᵢ are in different SCCs Cut Property (MST) A minimum-weight edge crossing any cut (S, S̄) is in some MST. Proof idea: If MST T doesn't contain e*, add e* (creates cycle), remove another cut edge e'. w(T') ≤ w(T). Ergodic Theorem (Markov Chains) Irreducible + aperiodic ⟹ unique stationary distribution π, and Pᵏ → π regardless of start. Edge Types in DFS (Directed Graphs) Type Direction post(z) vs post(w) Tree Parent → child post(z) > post(w) Back Descendant → ancestor post(z) < post(w) ⚠️ Forward Ancestor → descendant post(z) > post(w) Cross No ancestor relation post(z) > post(w) Only back edges have post(z) < post(w). Everything else: post(z) > post(w). SCC Algorithm — Quick Steps Build G^R (reverse edges) DFS(G^R) → get post numbers Order vertices by decreasing post number DFS(G) in that order → each Explore = one SCC 2-SAT — Quick Steps Build implication graph: clause (α ∨ β) → edges ᾱ→β and β̄→α Run SCC algorithm If any xᵢ and x̄ᵢ in same SCC → unsatisfiable Assign: sink SCC = TRUE, its complement (source) = FALSE, repeat MST — Quick Reminders Kruskal's: Sort edges, add lightest if no cycle (Union-Find) Prim's: Grow one tree, add lightest cut edge (min-heap) Both correct via Cut Property Trees: n-1 edges, connected, acyclic (any 2 ⟹ 3rd) PageRank — Quick Formula $$\pi(x) = \sum_{y \in in(x)} \frac{\pi(y)}{|out(y)|}$$ Equivalent to πP = π (stationary distribution) Damping: P' = αP + (1-α)·(1/N)·J, with α ≈ 0.85 Sink nodes: random jump (α = 0) Compute via power iteration: μ · P'^k → π ⚠️ Common Pitfalls Pitfall Why It's Wrong Using Dijkstra's with negative weights Can give wrong shortest paths; use Bellman-Ford Topological sort on a cyclic graph Impossible — check for back edges first Confusing 2-SAT implication direction (α ∨ β) gives ᾱ→β, NOT α→β Assuming MST is unique Multiple MSTs possible if edges have equal weights (total weight is unique) Assuming webgraph is ergodic It's not — damping parameter is essential Forgetting Markov chains need stochastic matrix Every row must sum to 1 🎯 Exam Tips DFS is the Swiss Army knife — SCCs, topo sort, cycle detection all build on it Cut Property proof — high probability exam question. Know the swap argument. 2-SAT construction — practice converting clauses to implication edges quickly SCC order matters — DFS on G^R first, then G. Not the other way around. Runtimes are all O(n+m) for graph decomposition algorithms (BFS, DFS, SCC, 2-SAT, topo sort). Only MST requires sorting: O(E log V). PageRank = stationary distribution — know the three equivalent views (citation, random walk, eigenvector)

title: Common Course Runtimes
text: Common Course Runtimes Category: Staff Use Only > Supplemental Material Date: 2026-05-18 05:34 Link: https://edstem.org/us/courses/98593/discussion/8077881 Pinned: True | Votes: 11 | Views: 1866 Post When performing runtime analysis for this course, we expect the analysis to be presented as the worst-case runtime analysis, presented in fully simplified Big-O notation. For example, O(2n + 3n) is expected to be presented as O(n). As most assessable material for this course is written, this post exists to present the runtimes associated with common actions you may describe in your algorithms. When no supporting details are presented for special handling in a submission, the default action (underlined) and associated runtimes will prevail. For the runtimes presented below, n represents the length or size of the object being used. ListIn the context of the course, we will treat lists as standard arrays, indexable by integers starting at 1. Creating a list (empty) O(1) Creating a list with initial contents O(n) Adding an item to a list (appends to the end) O(1) Adding an item to a specific non-tail location of a list O(n) Remove an item from a list (swap tail element and pop) O(1) Remove an item from the list while maintaining order O(n) Check the length of a list (via counting) O(n) Check if a list contains an element (via scanning) O(n) Hash Table / Hash Set / Map / SetWhile common and practical for software development, these data types have worst-case performance that generally makes them undesirable. Unless the problem gives you data in this format (typically as a set), you are unlikely to need this. Creating a hash table (empty) O(1) Creating a hash table with initial contents O(n^2) Adding an item to a hash table O(n) Remove an item from a hash table O(n) Check the length of a hash table (via counting) O(n) Check if a hash table contains an element (via scanning) O(n) GraphIn this course, we will always treat graphs as adjacency lists, with an indexable list of vertices each pointing to a linked list of adjacent vertices. You can assume you know n (number of vertices |V|) and m (number of edges |E|). Traversing a graph O(n+m) (Includes reversing, copying, subgraphing, or anything that requires working with all vertices and edges in a graph) Operating on a single vertex v O(1) (Includes find, read, removal) Operating on all (or a subset of) vertices V O(n) (Includes find, read, removal) Operating on a single edge e=(u,v) O(n) This is due to the need to traverse up to n vertices in the adjacency list for vertex u, to find and operate on adjacency v. (Includes find, read, removal) Operating on all edges |E| O(n+m) (Includes find, read, removal) Accessing properties of an edge or vertex O(1) This assumes you already have access to the vertex or edge itself - that is, you have paid the runtime costs above for finding that vertex/edge, (e.g. weight, color, cost, capacity, etc.) Special note about simplifications If you KNOW something about the connectivity of the graph (e.g. MST, Flow, or the problem states it), it is possible to simplify some runtimes: We know the graph is connected and m >= n-1 O(n+m) = O(m+m) = O(2m) = O(m) We know the graph is a clique and m = n(n-1)/2 O(n+m) = O(n+n^2) = O(n^2) Responses (4) Comment by User 1282049 on 2026-05-19 15:58 (Votes: 0) Hi, I was wondering if "remove an item" refers to an index in the List section, and "remove an item" refers to a value in the Hash Table section? For the list, I couldn't see how you would get O(1) removal without first knowing where the item is. For the hash table (assuming chaining), I'm guessing that with the worst possible hash function (100% collisions), you would need an O(n) scan of the collided bucket to find the item to remove. Comment by User 137215 on 2026-05-19 16:21 (Votes: 1) For the list, I couldn't see how you would get O(1) removal without first knowing where the item is. The operation assumes you have some index i you want to remove. Of course if you have to "find" that index i, that will first take O(n) which is what the post above says: Check if a list contains an element (via scanning) * O(n) Once you have i and you have n, then you can do what it says and we will consider it O(1). It is important to note that if you care about maintaining the remaining relative order, then you don't get this speedup. Comment by User 1015969 on 2026-05-20 13:21 (Votes: 0) Can you explain what "maintaining the remaining relative order" means? If you remove an element, the remaining elements in my mind still maintain their order. Ex. 1, 2, 3, 4 -> Remove 2 -> 1, 3, 4. The "order" is still ascending, or in other cases the "order" is the same minus a single element, but the river still flows Comment by User 215685 on 2026-05-20 23:20 (Votes: 0) you have to shift the other elements to not have the removed element there which is why removal is O(n) for arrays and O(1) for linked lists (assuming you have the pointer already). In practice some people will put a tombstone value for removals and won't have to shift all the other elements, but most often that's not the case. Comment by User 137215 on 2026-05-21 09:08 (Votes: 0) In practice some people will put a tombstone value for removals This can be a clever way for keeping order and performing removals. Unfortunately, the most common use of this is students picking a tombstone value that is also a valid input value... Comment by User 1282049 on 2026-05-21 04:39 (Votes: 0) want to remove the 2 1,2,3,4 step 1: swap with last 1,4,3,2 step 2: pop the tail element 1,4,3 congratulations, you have done an O(1) removal (but you decided to not care about relative order) Comment by User 649126 on 2026-05-22 07:47 (Votes: 1) Accessing T(n) in a 1-d list, let T(i), where 1<=i<=n, which is treated as an array we assume it takes O(1) and not O(n), right? Similarly, for 2-d lists, let T(i,j) for 1<=i, j <=n (treated as 2-d arrays) T(1,n) is extracted in O(1) and not O(n), correct? Thanks Comment by User 137215 on 2026-05-22 08:19 (Votes: 0) The DP tables follow the list runtimes above. If you go from 1D to 2D (n x n), then it goes from O(n) to O(n^2) and O(1) to O(1) for their respective operations. Comment by User 649126 on 2026-05-23 00:52 (Votes: 0) To summarize, Building/Creating a 1-D T(i) vector with 1<=i<=n takes 𝒪(n) Building/Creating a 2-D T(i,j) table with 1<=i<=n and 1<=j<=n takes 𝒪(n2) Accessing the i-th element of an 1-D T(i) vector with 1<=i<=n takes 𝒪(1) Accessing the (i-th, j-th) element a 2-D T(i,j) table with 1<=i<=n and 1<=j<=n takes 𝒪(1) Comment by User 281747 on 2026-05-22 11:43 (Votes: 0) Can we have this and the list of black box runtimes as reference on the exams? Comment by User 137215 on 2026-05-22 12:09 (Votes: 0) The problems on the exam will be presented similarly to how the problems are presented to you on the homework. Historically, the information you're asking for is not part of the exam 1 experience. Comment by User 281747 on 2026-05-23 09:38 (

title: Graph Theory Guidance
text: Graph Theory Guidance Category: Staff Use Only > Supplemental Material Date: 2026-06-15 17:47 Link: https://edstem.org/us/courses/98593/discussion/8129087 Pinned: True | Votes: 3 | Views: 1437 Post Below you will find information on how you should approach solving Graph Theory problems in this class. Feel free to ask questions about writing a Graph Theory solution in this thread. Context A graph theory problem is a form of reduction. You are given some problem to solve, and the challenge is to solve that problem by using one of the graph algorithms we study this semester. In other words, you reduce your problem to a graph problem such that a known algorithm can solve it for you. The known algorithms (#192) are treated as a black-box - you are not allowed to change / modify them in any way. You can (and often will) modify the original problem input in some way or create a graph which represents the input - this becomes the input to the black-box. You then use the outputs from the black-box (possibly with modification) to output a solution to the original problem. Rules NO PSEUDOCODE - YOU WILL LOSE A LOT OF POINTS You may only use known black box algorithms (#192). You must use one or more black box algorithms (#192) to solve problems. You must not modify any black box algorithms (#192). Re-creating a black box with modifications is the same thing. All graphs are in adjacency list format (see below) You can assume you always have n and m for purposes of runtime analysis, where n represents the number of vertices |V| and m represents the number of edges |E|. #192 isn't exactly relevant here, but it's worth another link. See #8 for common course runtimes, including operations performed to graphs. Required Parts of a Graph Theory Solution (a) Algorithm Describe "how" to solve the problem in words (narrative/paragraph form) Bulleted steps are fine, but pseudocode/code is not allowed "Pseudocode" includes line-by-line restatement of code as words. If you find yourself using a lot of nested bullets, you might be erring on the side of line-by-line conversion of code into words, which is not a narrative. Describe what modifications are performed to the input, if any Detail the black box algorithm(s) being used What is passed in as input What is being used from its output Describe what modifications are performed to the output, if any Repeat as needed for any other black boxes Detail the final return for the problem's solution (b) Justification of Correctness Describe "why" your algorithm solves the problem, in words (narrative). This is not a formal inductive proof; informal justification is ok. Explain how your problem is solved by the black boxes used Explain how changing the inputs and/or outputs gets what you want (c) Runtime Analysis Analyze all steps of the algorithm in Big-O notation. Always, always in this course - worst case Big-O. Can explain/analyze in words and assert known black-box runtimes without further justification. Must include run time analysis for any pre/post processing See #8 for common runtimes when dealing with graphs. Provide an overall final runtime in (you guessed it) Big-O notation, fully simplified. If the problem specifies a target runtime, you must meet that for full credit. If we do not specify a runtime, faster (and correct) in Big O notation are worth more credit. While we work with worse-case runtimes, we also work with tightest-bound runtimes. That is, even though the worst case for an O(n+m) operation occurs when m= n^2, it is incorrect to state O(n^2) for such an operation unless we know something about the graph (e.g., it is a clique). O(n+m) is the tightest worse-case bound on such an operation Similarly, if we know the graph is a tree, then m = n-1 and O(n+m) operations collapse to O(n). When conveying the well-known runtimes of usable black boxes, you are not required to simplify it, even if the graph's constraints would allow for it. Graph Representation Graphs in this class, unless stated otherwise: Are simple graphs (no multi-edges or self-loops) Are always given in adjacency list format (see DPV 3.1.1) An array of vertices that are pointers to linked lists of adjacent vertices, such that each element in the list represents an edge to another vertex. Vertices are assumed to be labeled in such a way where they can be found by indexing into the array. Can be connected or disconnected, undirected or directed, sparse or dense If unstated by the problem, you should assume any of these could be true Are connected and undirected when working with MSTs Are connected and directed when working with flow networks When working with graphs, there are a number of common actions that can be presented without needing to go into details. Some examples as follows: Reversing a graph: "Reverse the edges in the graph". Copying a graph: "Create a copy of G and call it GCopy". Creating subgraphs: "Create a copy of G called GEven that only keeps edges with even numbered weights". "Create a copy of G called GK that only keeps vertices with a degree > k". "Create a residual graph of G called Grs that uses the flows from our run of EK". Unless the task can't directly be completed in this runtime, each of these are assumed to be O(n + m) operations. Please ask questions regarding the expectations for Graph Theory problems in this thread. Responses (7) Comment by User 821283 on 2026-06-18 14:29 (Votes: 2) Is that ok if my algorithm include 1 (or more) black box algorithm, but has also other non-trivial steps that traverse the graph? As an example, the 2nd part of the algorithm below is not a black box, but the 1st part is. Is that acceptable? Examples for 3.16 (minimum number of semesters necessary to complete the curriculum) Run topological sort on the graph. Under topological order, find the first vertex with incoming edges, remove all vertices before that from the graph, and add one semester. Repeat this step until graph is empty. Comment by User 137215 on 2026-06-18 23:49 (Votes: 2) Yes, it's common that, as part of your solutions, there may be pre-processing or post-processing work that takes place around the black box usage. This could be creating subgraphs, modifying graphs, performing graph traversal, doing calculations, etc. Comment by User 813950 on 2026-06-19 08:41 (Votes: 0) I see that it says its graded; only this homework is graded? Comment by User 135758 on 2026-06-19 09:01 (Votes: 0) Is this for Homework 5? "Graded" generally means "marked for feedback". The weighting of those markings is unchanged from the syllabus. Comment by User 813950 on 2026-06-19 09:38 (Votes: 0) yes; sorry I got confused when i saw highlighted word graded. Thanks Comment by User 137215 on 2026-06-19 09:43 (Votes: 0) Yes. If you make a submission, the graders grade and so it goes. Comment by User 585876 on 2026-06-19 23:11 (Votes: 0) Just to clarify wording: Similarly, if we know the graph is a tree, then m = n-1 and O(n+m) operations collapse to O(n). Is this saying th

title: Usable Black Boxes for Graphs
text: Usable Black Boxes for Graphs Category: Staff Use Only > Supplemental Material Date: 2026-06-15 17:48 Link: https://edstem.org/us/courses/98593/discussion/8129088 Pinned: True | Votes: 5 | Views: 1850 Post For the graphs section, you must use the algorithms presented in the course materials to help you solve presented problems. That is, solutions in this section must use one (or a combo) of the black boxes to solve the presented problem. Providing full implementation details of one of these black boxes, and adding in a twist or in-line tracking will be treated as a modification.Many graphs algorithms exist beyond what is covered in this course; however, only those explicitly mentioned here may be used. The use of extra-curricular algorithms will result in penalties, including for variations of these algorithms outside of the lectures, book, and this post.Wikipedia entries are provided for supplemental reading, such as history and discovery of these algorithms. Where differences exist, this post represents the version used in this course.All runtimes below are presented assuming n = |vertices| and m = |edges|.All outputs below are produced as n-sized lists, containing an entry for each vertex in the graph, and are able to be indexed by vertex in O(1) time to read its contents, unless otherwise noted.The following black boxes are available for use during the remainder of the course: Depth-First Search (DFS) [see also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Depth-first_search]- Input: A simple graph in adjacency list format A starting vertex This is optional. If not provided, DFS will start from an arbitrary location. This is accomplished through adjacency list ordering, which we assume takes place when you provide a starting vertex. Internal (not accessible after run): visited[] - A list containing whether the indexed vertex was touched by DFS. All entries of this list will always be set to true when DFS completes. This is populated by Explore, an internal subroutine of DFS. Output: ccnum[] - A list containing the connected component number of the indexed vertex. Vertices reachable from the starting vertex will have a connected component number of 1. prev[] - A list containing the parent vertex of the indexed vertex, which can be used to create a path from the starting vertex to any reachable vertex. Unreachable vertices and the starting vertex have a parent of nil. pre[] - A list containing the pre-visit number for the indexed vertex. post[] - A list containing the post-visit number for the indexed vertex. Runtime: O(n + m) Implementation details: DPV pp. 83-91 GR1.3, GR1.6 Common uses: Finding connected components on a graph Finding a cycle in a graph Producing a path from starting vertex to a reachable vertex Topological Sort (see also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topological_sorting)- Input: A simple, directed, acyclic graph in adjacency list format Output: order[] - A list of vertices, sorted in topological ordering from source to sink. Note: This output is indexed numerically rather than by vertex. All outputs from the run of Depth-First Search (DFS) are also available to you. Runtime: O(n + m) Implementation details: DPV pp. 89-91 GR1.10 Common uses: Finding the topological sorting in a DAG Strongly Connected Components (SCC) [see also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strongly_connected_component]- Input: A simple, directed graph in adjacency list format Output: G_SCC = (V_SCC, E_SCC) - The strongly connected components metagraph, provided in adjacency list format. By construction, the metagraph is a DAG with vertices sorted from sink to source This ordering is known as reverse topological ordering. All outputs from the final run of Depth-First Search (DFS) are also available to you. This data is used to connect entities in the metagraph to the original input graph. For example, the first vertex in V_SCC represents all vertices with ccnum[v] = 1 from the DFS outputs from the original input graph. Runtime: O(n + m) Implementation details: DPV pp. 91-95 GR1.21 Common uses: Finding strongly connected components on a graph Particularly useful for finding source and sink SCCs Breadth-First Search (BFS) [see also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Breadth-first_search]- Input: A simple graph in adjacency list format A starting vertex Output: dist[] - A list containing the unweighted distance from the starting vertex to the indexed vertex for all vertices in the graph. Unreachable vertices have a distance of inf. prev[] - A list containing the parent vertex of the indexed vertex, which can be used to create a path from the starting vertex to any reachable vertex. Unreachable vertices and the starting vertex have a parent of nil. Runtime: O(n + m) Implementation details: DPV pp. 105-106 GR1.24 Common uses: Reachability analysis Unweighted shortest path determination Producing a path from starting vertex to a reachable vertex Dijkstra's (see also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dijkstra%27s_algorithm)- Input: A simple graph in adjacency list format A starting vertex A list of non-negative edge weights Output: dist[] - A list containing the weighted distance from the starting vertex to the indexed vertex for all vertices in the graph. Unreachable vertices have a distance of inf. prev[] - A list containing the parent vertex of the indexed vertex, which can be used to create a path from the starting vertex to any reachable vertex. Unreachable vertices and the starting vertex have a parent of nil. Runtime: O((n + m) log n) Implementation details: DPV pp. 109-115 GR1.24 Common uses: Reachability analysis Weighted shortest path determination Producing a path from starting vertex to a reachable vertex Bellman-Ford (BF) [see also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bellman%E2%80%93Ford_algorithm]- Input: A simple graph in adjacency list format A starting vertex A list of edge weights Output: dist[] - A list containing the weighted distance from the starting vertex to the indexed vertex for all vertices in the graph. Unreachable vertices have a distance of inf. Values are based on the n-1 th iteration. prev[] - A list containing the parent vertex of the indexed vertex, which can be used to create a path from the starting vertex to any reachable vertex. Unreachable vertices and the starting vertex have a parent of nil. iter[][] - A 2-dimensional list containing the first indexed iteration's shortest path from the starting vertex to the second indexed vertex. For example, iter[3][v] contains the distance from the starting vertex to v at the end of the 3rd iteration. This table contains iterations 0 through n. Runtime: O(nm) Implementation details: DPV pp. 115-119 DP3.6 Common uses: Reachability analysis Weighted shortest path determination Negative cycle detection Producing a path from starting vertex to a reachable vertex Floyd-Warshall (FW) [see also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Floyd%E2%80%93Warshall_algorithm]- Input: A simple graph in adjacency list format A list of edge weights Output: dist[][

title: Week 5 — Minimum Spanning Tree
text: Week 5 — Minimum Spanning Tree 2026-06-15 → 06-21 · Module GR3 (MST: Kruskal's & Prim's) ◀ W04 Graphs · All weeks · Index · W06 Max-Flow ▶ Due this week Item Type Note HW5 written MST problems Quiz 4 format graph-theory answer format Watch the lecture (MST) ▶ Open video · Transcript Learning objectives State and prove the Cut Property (exchange argument). Trace Kruskal's (sort + Union-Find) and Prim's (grow + min-heap). Explain why both algorithms are special cases of the Cut Property. Quote the O(Elog V) runtime and where the log comes from. Core concepts Spanning tree & MST Definition. A spanning tree of a connected undirected graph G = (V, E) is a subset of edges that is connected and acyclic and touches every vertex. A minimum spanning tree (MST) is a spanning tree of minimum total edge weight. Intuition: the cheapest way to wire all vertices together with no redundant loops. Key facts: A tree on n vertices has exactly n − 1 edges. Any two of {connected, acyclic, n − 1 edges} imply the third. Adding one edge to a tree creates exactly one cycle; removing one edge from a tree disconnects it into exactly two components. MST need not be unique — but if all edge weights are distinct, the MST is unique. Cuts Definition. A cut (S, V \ S) is any partition of the vertices into two non-empty sets. An edge crosses the cut if its endpoints are on opposite sides. This is the object the Cut Property reasons about — every greedy MST step is secretly "pick the lightest edge across some cut." The greedy / exchange idea Both MST algorithms are greedy: repeatedly commit to the locally-cheapest safe edge and never backtrack. Correctness comes from the Cut Property, proved by an exchange argument (swap a heavier edge out for the greedy edge without increasing total weight). Union-Find (disjoint sets) Purpose. Kruskal needs to test "do these two endpoints already sit in the same tree component?" fast. Union-Find supports find(x) (which component) and union(x,y) (merge) in near-constant amortized time with path compression + union by rank (O(α(n)), effectively O(1)). Algorithms Kruskal's — O(Elog V) Sort all edges by weight ascending. (this sort dominates: O(Elog E) = O(Elog V)) Initialize Union-Find with each vertex in its own set; start with an empty MST. Scan edges cheapest-first. For edge (u, v): if find(u) ≠ find(v), add it to the MST and union(u,v); otherwise skip (it would create a cycle). Stop once the MST has n − 1 edges. Use when: the graph is given as an edge list / is sparse; easy to reason about. Prim's — O(Elog V) Pick any start vertex; put it in the tree set T. Maintain a min-heap of edges crossing from T to the rest (or of vertex keys = the lightest edge connecting each outside vertex to T). Repeatedly extract the lightest crossing edge whose far endpoint is outside T; add that edge and vertex to T; update keys of its neighbours. Stop when T = V. Use when: the graph is given as an adjacency list / you want to grow one connected tree. Algorithm Strategy Data structure Runtime Kruskal's add globally-lightest edge that doesn't form a cycle Union-Find O(Elog V) Prim's grow one tree, add lightest edge leaving it min-heap O(Elog V) Both reach O(Elog V); the log V is the sort (Kruskal) or the heap operations (Prim). Key theorems & results Cut Property For any cut (S, V \ S), if e is a strictly lightest edge crossing the cut, then e belongs to every MST. (For ties: e belongs to some MST.) Proof (exchange argument). Suppose an MST T does not contain the lightest crossing edge e = (u, v). Adding e to T creates a cycle; since u ∈ S and v ∈ V \ S, that cycle must cross the cut a second time on some edge e′. By choice of e, w(e) ≤ w(e′). Form T′ = T − e′ + e: still spanning and acyclic, with w(T′) = w(T) − w(e′) + w(e) ≤ w(T). So T′ is also an MST and it contains e. ◼ Consequences. Kruskal applies the Cut Property to the cut separating the two components its candidate edge would join; Prim applies it to the cut (T, V \ T). Both only ever add lightest-across-a-cut edges, so they only add MST edges. Worked example (Kruskal trace) Vertices A, B, C, D, E; edges (weight): AB(1), BC(2), BD(3), AC(4), CD(5), DE(6). Step Edge Weight Action 1 AB 1 add — different sets 2 BC 2 add — different sets 3 BD 3 add — different sets 4 AC 4 skip — A, C already connected (cycle) 5 CD 5 skip — C, D already connected 6 DE 6 add — connects E; now n − 1 = 4 edges, done MST = {AB, BC, BD, DE}, total weight 1 + 2 + 3 + 6 = 12. Common pitfalls / exam traps Forgetting MST is for undirected graphs; "MST of a directed graph" is a different (arborescence) problem, out of scope. Claiming uniqueness without the distinct-weights hypothesis. In Kruskal, skipping the cycle check — adding an edge inside a component breaks the tree. Quoting O(E) for the algorithms — the sort / heap makes it O(Elog V). How to answer (HW / exam) MST is a black box: cite Kruskal/Prim and their O(Elog V) runtimes; invoke the Cut Property for correctness rather than re-deriving from scratch. See graph guidance. Go deeper — readings · guidance · deep notes DPV §5.1 (greedy / MST) Graph guidance · Black boxes · Common runtimes Deep notes: GR3 MST · cheat sheet lectures/ · transcripts/ · readings/ · guidance/ · notes/ Comprehensive Notes Full lecture notes for GR3, with figures and diagrams inline. MST is our first greedy algorithm that provably works; the key is the Cut Property. GR3 — Minimum Spanning Tree 1. Problem definition Given: an undirected, connected graph G = (V, E) with edge weights w(e). Find: a spanning tree T ⊆ E of minimum total weight min ∑e ∈ Tw(e). A spanning tree of G is a subset of edges that connects all vertices (spans the graph), has no cycles (is a tree), and has exactly |V|−1 edges. 2. Tree properties These three properties are equivalent for a subgraph with n vertices: Property Implies Connected + n − 1 edges must be a tree Connected + acyclic must have n − 1 edges n − 1 edges + acyclic must be connected Also: a tree has exactly one path between any pair of vertices; removing any edge disconnects it; adding any edge creates exactly one cycle. Intuition: a tree is the most economical way to keep a graph connected (n − 1 edges); an MST does so with minimum weight. 3. Kruskal's algorithm Repeatedly add the lightest edge that doesn't create a cycle. Kruskal(G, w): // with Union-Find 1. Sort E by increasing weight 2. X = ∅ 3. Initialize Union-Find with each vertex in its own set 4. For each edge e = (v, w) in sorted order: if Find(v) ≠ Find(w): X = X ∪ {e} Union(v, w) 5. Return X Cycle detection with Union-Find. A disjoint-set structure supports Find(v) (component of v) and Union(v,w) (merge components). Adding edge (v, w) creates a cycle ⇔ Find(v) = Find(w). Runtime: O(|E|log |V|) — sorting is O(|E|log |E|) = O(|E|log |V|) since |E| ≤ |V|2; Union-Find costs O(|E|⋅α(|V|)) ≈ O(|E|); sorting dominates. Worked example graph: graph LR A ---|1| B A ---|4| C B ---|2| C B ---|3| D C ---|5| D C ---|7| E D ---|6| E Sorted edges: (A,

title: GR3: Minimum Spanning Tree
text: GR3: Minimum Spanning Tree 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 5.1 (Greedy Algorithms — MST) MST is our first greedy algorithm that provably works. The key is the Cut Property. 1. Problem Definition Given: An undirected, connected graph G = (V, E) with edge weights w(e) Find: A spanning tree T ⊆ E of minimum total weight: min Σ w(e) for e ∈ T A spanning tree of G is a subset of edges that: Connects all vertices (spans the graph) Has no cycles (it's a tree) Has exactly |V| - 1 edges 2. Tree Properties These three properties are equivalent for a subgraph with n vertices: Property Meaning Connected + n-1 edges → Must be a tree Connected + acyclic → Must have n-1 edges n-1 edges + acyclic → Must be connected Also important: A tree has exactly one path between any pair of vertices Removing any edge disconnects a tree Adding any edge to a tree creates exactly one cycle 💡 Intuition: A tree is the most economical way to keep a graph connected — it uses the minimum number of edges (n-1). An MST does this with minimum total weight. 3. Kruskal's Algorithm Repeatedly add the lightest edge that doesn't create a cycle. Kruskal(G, w): Input: Undirected graph G = (V, E) with weights w(e) Output: MST edge set X 1. Sort E by increasing weight 2. X = ∅ 3. For each edge e = (v, w) in sorted order: if adding e to X doesn't create a cycle: X = X ∪ {e} 4. Return X Cycle Detection with Union-Find How do we efficiently check "does adding (v, w) create a cycle"? Use a Union-Find (disjoint set) data structure: Find(v): Returns the component containing v Union(v, w): Merges the components of v and w Adding edge (v, w) creates a cycle ⟺ Find(v) = Find(w) (already in same component). Kruskal(G, w): // with Union-Find 1. Sort E by increasing weight 2. X = ∅ 3. Initialize Union-Find with each vertex in its own set 4. For each edge e = (v, w) in sorted order: if Find(v) ≠ Find(w): X = X ∪ {e} Union(v, w) 5. Return X Runtime: O(|E| log |V|) Sorting: O(|E| log |E|) = O(|E| log |V|) since |E| ≤ |V|² Union-Find operations: O(|E| · α(|V|)) ≈ O(|E|) (nearly constant per op) Dominated by sorting Worked Example graph LR A ---|1| B A ---|4| C B ---|2| C B ---|3| D C ---|5| D C ---|7| E D ---|6| E Sorted edges: (A,B,1), (B,C,2), (B,D,3), (A,C,4), (C,D,5), (D,E,6), (C,E,7) Step Edge Weight Add? Reason 1 (A,B) 1 ✅ Different components 2 (B,C) 2 ✅ Different components 3 (B,D) 3 ✅ Different components 4 (A,C) 4 ❌ Same component — would create cycle 5 (C,D) 5 ❌ Same component 6 (D,E) 6 ✅ Different components. Done! (4 edges = n-1) MST edges: {(A,B), (B,C), (B,D), (D,E)} with total weight = 1+2+3+6 = 12 graph LR A ---|1| B B ---|2| C B ---|3| D D ---|6| E style A fill:#90EE90 style B fill:#90EE90 style C fill:#90EE90 style D fill:#90EE90 style E fill:#90EE90 4. The Cut Property — Why Kruskal's Works What Is a Cut? Definition: For an undirected graph G = (V, E), a cut is a partition of V into two non-empty sets S and S̄ = V \ S. The cut edges are: cut(S, S̄) = {(v, w) ∈ E : v ∈ S, w ∈ S̄} graph LR subgraph S_set["S"] A B end subgraph Sbar["S-bar"] C D E end A ---|"cut edge"| C B ---|"cut edge"| D A --- B C --- D D --- E The Cut Property Lemma Lemma (Cut Property): Let X ⊆ E be a subset of some MST T. Let S ⊆ V be such that no edge of X crosses the cut (S, S̄). Let e* be a minimum weight edge crossing the cut. Then X ∪ {e*} ⊆ T' for some MST T'. In plain English: A minimum-weight edge across any cut is safe to add to the MST. 💡 Intuition: If I give you any tree T that doesn't include e*, I can swap e* in and some other cut edge out, and the weight won't increase (because e* is the lightest across the cut). So there's always an MST containing e*. Proof of the Cut Property Setup: Fix G, X, T (an MST containing X), and S. e* is a min-weight edge in cut(S, S̄) No edge of X crosses the cut Goal: Show X ∪ {e*} ⊆ T' for some MST T'. Case A: e* ∈ T. Then T' = T. Done. ✅ Case B: e* ∉ T. We construct T'. Add e* to T → creates a unique cycle C (since T is a tree) e* = (a, b) crosses the cut (a ∈ S, b ∈ S̄). The cycle C must cross the cut at least one more time (to get back from S̄ to S). Let e' be another edge of C that crosses the cut. Set T' = T ∪ {e*} \ {e'} Claim 1: T' is a tree. T' has n-1 edges (added one, removed one) T' is connected: any path in T that used e' can detour through the rest of cycle C (which includes e*) Connected + n-1 edges → tree ✅ Claim 2: T' is an MST. w(e*) ≤ w(e') because e* is minimum weight across the cut and e' also crosses the cut w(T') = w(T) + w(e*) - w(e') ≤ w(T) But T is already minimum weight, so w(T') = w(T) ✅ Claim 3: X ∪ {e} ⊆ T'.* X ⊆ T and X has no edges crossing the cut, so e' ∉ X, meaning X ⊆ T' = T \ {e'} ∪ {e*} e* ∈ T' by construction ✅ 🎯 Exam Tip: The proof of the Cut Property is one of the most likely proof questions on the exam. Know the two cases (e* in T or not) and the swap argument. 5. Why Kruskal's Is Correct (via Cut Property) At each step of Kruskal's, when we add edge e = (v, w): v and w are in different components (otherwise we'd skip) Let S = the component containing v, S̄ = everything else No edge of X (edges added so far) crosses this cut (by definition of components) e is the minimum weight edge crossing this cut (because we process edges in sorted order — all lighter edges either don't cross this cut or were already added) By the Cut Property, adding e is safe ✅ By induction, X is always a subset of some MST. When |X| = n-1, X IS that MST. 6. Prim's Algorithm Prim's grows a single tree from a starting vertex, always adding the lightest edge connecting the tree to a non-tree vertex. Prim(G, w): Input: Undirected graph G = (V, E) with weights w(e) Output: MST edge set X X = ∅ S = {any starting vertex} while |X| < |V| - 1: e* = minimum weight edge in cut(S, V\S) X = X ∪ {e*} S = S ∪ {endpoint of e* in V\S} Return X Implementation: Use a min-heap keyed by the lightest edge connecting each non-tree vertex to the tree. Runtime: O(|E| log |V|) with binary heap Correctness: Direct application of the Cut Property at each step (S = tree vertices so far, no edge of X leaves S since the tree is contained in S). Prim's Example Same graph as before, starting from A: graph LR A ---|1| B A ---|4| C B ---|2| C B ---|3| D C ---|5| D C ---|7| E D ---|6| E Step S (tree) Lightest cut edge Add 1 {A} (A,B) w=1 (A,B) 2 {A,B} (B,C) w=2 (B,C) 3 {A,B,C} (B,D) w=3 (B,D) 4 {A,B,C,D} (D,E) w=6 (D,E) Same MST: {(A,B), (B,C), (B,D), (D,E)}, weight = 12 ✅ 7. Kruskal's vs Prim's Kruskal's Prim's Strategy Global: lightest edge overall Local: lightest edge from current tree Grows Forest → single tree Single tree from start Data structure Union-Find Min-heap Runtime O(|E| log |V|) O(|E| log |V|) Better for Sparse graphs Dense graphs Correctness Cut Property (S = component) Cut Property (S = tree so far) Both are just special cases of the generic "greedy + Cut Property" approach. ⚠️ Common Pitfall: MST is for undirected graphs only. The directed analog (minimum spanning arborescence) is a completely different prob

looks pretty good to me) https://gtalgorithms.wordpress.com/ Old course on youtube, Looks awesome!! https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLAwxTw4SYaPkbWSEj_1iO7rILlWDJImW4 Textbooks Official Algorithms by Dasgupta, C. H. Papadimitriou, and U. V. Vazirani http://algorithmics.lsi.upc.edu/docs/Dasgupta-Papadimitriou-Vazirani.pdf Secondary Algorithm Design by J. Kleinberg and E. Tardos https://ict.iitk.ac.in/wp-content/uploads/CS345-Algorithms-II-Algorithm-Design-by-Jon-Kleinberg-Eva-Tardos.pdf Lecture/Videos https://omscs.gatech.edu/cs-6515-graduate-algorithms-course-videos Topics Covered DP: Dynamic Programming RA: Randomized Algorithms DC: Divide and Conquer GR: Graph Algorithm MF: MaxFlow Problems LP: Linear Programming NP: Nondeterministic Polynomial time Problems Other Resources MIT: https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-006-introduction-to-algorithms-fall-2011/resources/lecture-videos/ UWashington: https://courses.cs.washington.edu/courses/cse417/ UIllinois: https://mfleck.cs.illinois.edu/building-blocks/index-sp2020.html UIllinois: https://jeffe.cs.illinois.edu/teaching/algorithms/#notes Schedule W01 - Dynamic Programming (Lectures: DP1) W02 - Dynamic Programming (Lectures: DP2, DP3) W03 - Divide and Conquer I (Lectures: DC1, DC3, DC4) W04 - Divide and Conquer II (Lectures: DC5, DC2) Exam 1 (W01-W04) W05 - Modular Arithmetic and RSA (Lectures: RA1, RA2) W06 - Graph Algorithm I (Lectures: GR1,GR2) W07 - Graph Algorithm II and Max Flow I (Lectures: GR3,MF1) W08 - Max Flow II (Lectures: MF2,MF3,MF5) W09 - Max Flow III (Lectures: MF4) Exam 2 (W05-W09) W10 - Np Completeness (Lectures: NP1,NP2,NP3) W11 - Spring Break W12 - Linear Programming (Lectures: LP1,LP2,LP3) W13 - NP and LP (Lectures: LP4 & NP4) W14 - More Complexity (Lectures: NP5) Exam 3 (W10-W13) W15 - Markov Chains W16 - Final Week Final Exam - Cumulative - Optional Grading 14% Homework 7% Coding Projects 7% Mini-Quizzes 3% Logistic Quizzes 69% Exams 23% a piece A [85-100%] B [70-85%] C [50-70%] Refresher Pseudo Code There doesn't appear to be a definitive guide to pseudo code. In the loosest sense of the phrase it is somewhere between mathematical notation and code. The goal is to describe an algorithm such that any programmer can understand it, and implement it, without needing to know a particular programming language. Of course what this means is that algorithm written using constructs found only in some programming language would automatically be dis-qualified. ie range(0,5) is found in python and should not be used. There are some exceptions to this, sum/min/max are so prevalent that there existence is virtually guaranteed. Thus they are allowed in this class. Another interesting tip to use the word as a function. length(A) would be allowed as it's meaning can be inferred, while A.len() would not be allowed. Dot Notation What is also not allowed is any form of dot notation. ie T.max(), S.sum() etc etc. This is unique to OOP style and is not found in many languages. But max(T), max(T[⋅]) are both allowed. Scoping Is done using indentation, not using {} Array Indexing can be done using either () or [] however array indexing like A[3:5] is not allowed nor are little tricks like A[-1] Checking Allowed: equality(==,=) and inequality(!=,<>) BAD Examples Good examples # This is basically python for i in range(1,n+1): for i = 1 to n t[i] += 1 t[i] = t[i] + 1 # This looks like C/C++ for( i=1; i<=n; i++ ){ for i = 1 -> n t[i]++ ; t[i] = t[i] + 1 } for i = 1...n t[i] = t[i] + 1 # -1 indexing is # not language agnostic # returns the last element return T[-1] return T[n] # dot notation not allowed # simple functions allowed x = A.size() x = length(A) y = x.len() Each algorithm is graded on four parts: a. Def

title: 📝 Graphs Cheat Sheet — CS 6515
text: 📝 Graphs Cheat Sheet — CS 6515 Algorithms at a Glance Algorithm Input Output Runtime Key Idea BFS G, source s dist[], prev[] O(n+m) Queue; shortest paths (unweighted) Dijkstra's G, s, w(e)>0 dist[], prev[] O((V+E)log V) Min-heap BFS; positive weights only DFS G pre[], post[], ccnum[] O(n+m) Recursion; foundation for everything Topo Sort DAG ordered vertices O(n+m) DFS, sort by decreasing post number SCC Directed G SCC labels O(n+m) DFS on G^R, then DFS on G 2-SAT 2-CNF formula assignment or NO O(n+m) Implication graph + SCC Kruskal's Undirected G, weights MST O(E log V) Sort edges; Union-Find Prim's Undirected G, weights MST O(E log V) Grow tree; min-heap Key Lemmas & Theorems Cycle Detection G has a cycle ⟺ DFS tree has a back edge SCC Source Lemma Vertex with highest post number (in DFS on G^R) is in a source SCC of G^R = sink SCC of G 2-SAT Lemma f is satisfiable ⟺ ∀i, xᵢ and x̄ᵢ are in different SCCs Cut Property (MST) A minimum-weight edge crossing any cut (S, S̄) is in some MST. Proof idea: If MST T doesn't contain e*, add e* (creates cycle), remove another cut edge e'. w(T') ≤ w(T). Ergodic Theorem (Markov Chains) Irreducible + aperiodic ⟹ unique stationary distribution π, and Pᵏ → π regardless of start. Edge Types in DFS (Directed Graphs) Type Direction post(z) vs post(w) Tree Parent → child post(z) > post(w) Back Descendant → ancestor post(z) < post(w) ⚠️ Forward Ancestor → descendant post(z) > post(w) Cross No ancestor relation post(z) > post(w) Only back edges have post(z) < post(w). Everything else: post(z) > post(w). SCC Algorithm — Quick Steps Build G^R (reverse edges) DFS(G^R) → get post numbers Order vertices by decreasing post number DFS(G) in that order → each Explore = one SCC 2-SAT — Quick Steps Build implication graph: clause (α ∨ β) → edges ᾱ→β and β̄→α Run SCC algorithm If any xᵢ and x̄ᵢ in same SCC → unsatisfiable Assign: sink SCC = TRUE, its complement (source) = FALSE, repeat MST — Quick Reminders Kruskal's: Sort edges, add lightest if no cycle (Union-Find) Prim's: Grow one tree, add lightest cut edge (min-heap) Both correct via Cut Property Trees: n-1 edges, connected, acyclic (any 2 ⟹ 3rd) PageRank — Quick Formula $$\pi(x) = \sum_{y \in in(x)} \frac{\pi(y)}{|out(y)|}$$ Equivalent to πP = π (stationary distribution) Damping: P' = αP + (1-α)·(1/N)·J, with α ≈ 0.85 Sink nodes: random jump (α = 0) Compute via power iteration: μ · P'^k → π ⚠️ Common Pitfalls Pitfall Why It's Wrong Using Dijkstra's with negative weights Can give wrong shortest paths; use Bellman-Ford Topological sort on a cyclic graph Impossible — check for back edges first Confusing 2-SAT implication direction (α ∨ β) gives ᾱ→β, NOT α→β Assuming MST is unique Multiple MSTs possible if edges have equal weights (total weight is unique) Assuming webgraph is ergodic It's not — damping parameter is essential Forgetting Markov chains need stochastic matrix Every row must sum to 1 🎯 Exam Tips DFS is the Swiss Army knife — SCCs, topo sort, cycle detection all build on it Cut Property proof — high probability exam question. Know the swap argument. 2-SAT construction — practice converting clauses to implication edges quickly SCC order matters — DFS on G^R first, then G. Not the other way around. Runtimes are all O(n+m) for graph decomposition algorithms (BFS, DFS, SCC, 2-SAT, topo sort). Only MST requires sorting: O(E log V). PageRank = stationary distribution — know the three equivalent views (citation, random walk, eigenvector)
