title: A Conceptual Approach to Solving Recurrences
text: A Conceptual Approach to Solving Recurrences Category: Staff Use Only > Supplemental Material Date: 2026-05-31 05:51 Link: https://edstem.org/us/courses/98593/discussion/8103813 Pinned: False | Votes: 38 | Views: 1599 Post This is a post from Christian, one of our former Head TAs. We refer to the below intuitive method as "Christian's Method."For students who don't like memorizing seemingly arbitrary formulas, here's a quick crash course on the conceptual approach to solving recurrences that are covered by the Master Theorem. I developed these concepts by solving several recurrences by hand until I noticed patterns, and since then have never had to look back at the Master Theorem--I truly don't know it. I personally strongly prefer this approach because I find conceptual understanding to be much more enduring and meaningful as a learner. Of course your learning style may lean towards memorizing formulae, and that's fine too! The runtime of a recurrence conceptually comes from two places: work due to subproblem proliferation vs. work done to merge subproblem results. For example, in the recurrence T(n) = 4T(n/2) + O(n), the 4T(n/2) characterizes the work due to subproblem proliferation, and the O(n) characterizes the work done to merge subproblem results. (Note that I use the term "merge" rather loosely here to describe whatever postprocessing work is performed on the subproblem results at each layer of recursion, such as the merge operation in mergesort.) Part 1: Determining work due to subproblem proliferation. This is probably best illustrated with examples. T(n) = 2T(n/2) - two subproblems, half the size, just as much work as before. T(n) = 5T(n/5) T(n) = 8T(n/8) All of those are O(n), since we made the subproblems smaller, but still had the same total amount of work. Here are several problems with quadratic subproblem proliferation; the problems got smaller, but we ended up with more of them: T(n) = 4T(n/2) T(n) = 9T(n/3) T(n) = 100T(n/10) Those are all O(n2) since the total amount of work to be done is growing quadratically. T(n) = 8T(n/2) T(n) = 64T(n/4) T(n) = 125T(n/5) Those are all O(n3). The problems got smaller, but we ended up with way more of them. Etc. It could be a non-integer exponent, and that's when we need logs. Consider the middle one below. T(n) = 4T(n/2) → O(n2) T(n) = 7T(n/2) T(n) = 8T(n/2) → O(n3) We could estimate it to be O(n2.8), which is pretty close. We could more accurately say it's O(nlog27), since that is, by definition, the exponent that turns 2 into 7. This even works if we get less subproblems than the linear case: T(n) = 2T(n/3) → O(nlog3​2) ≈ O(n0.631) Of course, if we only end up with a single problem at each step, we'll shrink down to a single unit of work: T(n) = T(n/2) → O(1) The above is probably the hardest part of this. There are now three possibilities when you compare the work from subproblem proliferation with the work from merging (postprocessing) the results from the subproblems into the final answer: The work due to subproblem proliferation dominates--the vast majority of the work occurs at the lowest level of recursion. Keep this dominant runtime as the overall runtime. The work due to merging results dominates--the vast majority of the work occurs at the highest level of recursion. Keep this dominant runtime as the overall runtime. The two tie--each level of O(log n) levels of recursion is the same amount of work, so the work at each level is multiplied by log n to get the overall result.* Here are a few overall examples: T(n) = 8T(n/2) + O(n2) Well that's O(n3) from subproblem proliferation vs. O(n2) for merge, and the former dominates. It's O(n3) overall. T(n) = 3T(n/4) + O(n) That's O(nlog43) ≈ O(n0.792) vs. O(n), so O(n) dominates. T(n) = 25T(n/5) + O(n2) This example has O(n2) work due to subproblem proliferation and O(n2) work due to merging subproblem results. Since it's a tie, this works out to O(n2log n). Hope this conceptual crash course helps someone! Feel free, as always, to ask clarifying follow ups. There is a niche edge case that falls into the "tie" category. O(nk) from subproblem proliferation and O(nklog n) from merge are close enough to tie, leading to O(nklog nlog n) runtime based on the tie case described in the original post. Responses (2) Comment by User 649238 on 2026-06-02 01:09 (Votes: 2) Thanks for this post. Could you also add recurrence when we are not splitting data but something else? For example, T(K) = T(K/2) + O(n) where the work done is O(n) at each level but we are splitting some other parameter (E.g. another input range or a value we need to determine). In that case, the second term is a function k (where n is treated as a constant). This resolves to O(n log K). Comment by User 1295220 on 2026-06-02 08:26 (Votes: 2) I think this would fall into the "Tie" rule. A more general description of the tie rule be: If all levels of the recursion tree do the same amount of work, then you have to count all of them. In other words, we can only ignore the middle work if either the top or bottom level dominates. In the example you presented, there is only one node at each level of the recursion tree. Each of those nodes does O(n) work. The total height of this tree is log(K). So we can multiply the total number of levels (the height) of the tree by the work done at each level to get the result. Comment by User 1295220 on 2026-06-02 08:34 (Votes: 6) This is extremely helpful. I have taken a handful of courses that go over recurrence relations at different universities, and this is by far the best explanation I have seen. Having the intuition is much better than the Master Theorem itself.

title: Usable D&C Algorithms
text: Usable D&C Algorithms Category: Staff Use Only > Supplemental Material Date: 2026-05-25 15:20 Link: https://edstem.org/us/courses/98593/discussion/8093303 Pinned: False | Votes: 8 | Views: 3834 Post For the divide and conquer section, you may use the algorithms presented in the course materials to help you solve presented problems. It may also be that the problem does not lend itself directly to one (or a combo) of the course algorithms, so you may choose to: Leverage divide and conquer techniques directly, avoiding the use of course algorithms; or, Make modifications to one or more of the course algorithms to make it fit the problem at hand Any modifications made to the course algorithms must be described and have the impact of the changes accounted for in the runtime analysis. Modifications listed below under "common modifications" do not need to be described in depth, but the use of a modified algorithm must be explicitly stated and an impact analysis must still be accounted for in the runtime analysis. Many divide and conquer algorithms exist beyond what is covered in this course; however, only those explicitly mentioned here may be used. The use of extra-curricular algorithms will result in penalties.Wikipedia entries are provided for supplemental reading, such as history and discovery of these algorithms. Where differences exist, this post represents the version used in this course.The following algorithms are available for use during the remainder of the course: Binary Search (see also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_search_algorithm)- Input: A sorted list of comparable elements. A starting index. 1 is assumed if not provided. An ending index. The size of the list (i.e., n) is assumed if the value is known. The target value. Output: The index of the first encountered match if one exists or "NOT FOUND" if no match could be found. Runtime: O(log n) Reference implementation: binary_search(A, s=1, n, T) L = s R = n while L ≤ R do m = floor((L + R) / 2) if A[m] < T L = m + 1 else if A[m] > T R = m − 1 else return m return unsuccessful Common modifications: Finding the leftmost matching value. Finding the rightmost matching value. Finding the insertion point for a missing matching value. Merge Sort (see also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Merge_sort)- Input: A list of comparable elements. A starting index. 1 is assumed if not provided. An ending index. The size of the list (i.e., n) is assumed if the value is known. Output: The original list, sorted in ascending order. Runtime: O(n log n) Reference implementation (based on DPV pp. 50-51): merge_sort(A, s=1, n) if n > 1 return merge(merge_sort(A[s ... n/2]), merge_sort(A[n/2 + 1 ... n])) else return A merge(L[1 ... i], R[1 ... j]) if i = 0 return R if j = 0 return L if L[1] ≤ R[1] return L[1] + merge(L[2 ... i], R[1 ... j]) else return R[1] + merge(L[1 ... i], R[2 ... j]) Common modifications: Returning the list in descending order. Fast Select (see also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Selection_algorithm)- Input: A list of comparable elements. A starting index. 1 is assumed if not provided. An ending index. The size of the list (i.e., n) is assumed if the value is known. An integer k Output: The kth smallest element from the original list. Runtime: O(n) Reference implementation (based on DC2.12): fast_select(A, s=1, n, k) if n = 1 return A[1] i = s size = 0 medians = [] while (i + 4) ≤ n do medians += merge_sort(A, i, i+4)[3] size += 1 i += 5 if i ≤ n medians += merge_sort(A, i, n)[ceil((n - i + 1) / 2)] size += 1 pivot = fast_select(medians, size / 2) lows = [] mids = [] highs = [] lowSize = 0 midSize = 0 for i = s to n if A[i] < pivot lows += A[i] lowSize += 1 else if A[i] > pivot highs += A[i] else mids += A[i] midSize += 1 if k ≤ lowSize return fast_select(lows, k) else if k > lowSize + midSize return fast_select(highs, k - lowSize - midSize) else return pivot Common modifications: Return the kth largest element. Fast Multiply (see also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karatsuba_algorithm)- Input: An n-bit integer x such that n = 2^k. An n-bit integer y such that n = 2^k. Output: The product of x and y. Runtime: O(n^(log``2`` 3)) Reference implementation (based on DPV p. 47, DC1.10) fast_multiply(x[1 ... n], y[1 ... n]) if n = 1 return x * y xl = extract_bits(x, 1, n/2) xr = extract_bits(x, n/2 + 1, n) yl = extract_bits(y, 1, n/2) yr = extract_bits(y, n/2 + 1, n) A = fast_multiply(xl, yl) B = fast_multiply(xr, yr) C = fast_multiply(xl + xr, yl + yr) return 2^n * A + 2^(n/2) * (C - A - B) + B Common modifications: None. Responses (4) Comment by User 817332 on 2026-05-26 07:22 (Votes: 1) Should we / are we supposed to memorize these algorithms? Comment by User 137215 on 2026-05-26 09:27 (Votes: 1) You are expected to know, know how to use, and know how to modify any of the algorithms above. Comment by User 1314770 on 2026-05-28 05:02 (Votes: 0) Does this include full reference implementation or just the items presented in #9 (no code/pseudocode) ? Comment by User 137215 on 2026-05-28 08:02 (Votes: 2) How you think about the internals is up to you. We've presented them as pseudocode above, as a way to concisely share the implementation details. In your submission, you would used words, and would modify them with words. As an example: I will modify the FastMultiply algorithm for this problem. Instead of splitting each n-bit integer into an right and left half, I will split it into four parts: upper right, lower right, upper left, lower left. The recursive pair-wise calls are replaced with [some new grouping using the 4-part split]. And for the return calculation, it will now be [some fancy math here]. In order to be able to give those details to make the modification, you will need to know how the FastMultiply algorithm works internally, to detail the changes you plan to make. And then, in the correctness, you'll cover why that modification allows you to correctly solve the original problem, along with whatever pre- and post-steps you perofrm. And finally, your runtime analysis will include the impact of the changes you've made. For example: Splitting the n-bit integer into 4 sets of n/4 bits instead of 2 sets of n/2 bits still only requires a single pass of the bits, so this step remains O(n). Comment by User 977644 on 2026-05-28 05:38 (Votes: 13) You don't need to memorize the code necessarily, you need to "memorize" something like this: "binary search takes a sorted array A and a target t. You keep two indices, L and R, that define the current search range. Each step you look at the middle index, mid. If A[mid] == t, you return it. If A[mid] < t, you move L to mid + 1; otherwise you move R to mid - 1. This process keeps discarding half the remaining range until it is empty or you find the target." Then you need to know and explain why binary search is O(log n) time. That's the basic idea, but like Jamie said, you should know the algorithms above well enough to apply variations to them to solve problems that are

title: Dynamic Programming Recurrence Relations
text: Dynamic Programming Recurrence Relations Category: Staff Use Only > Supplemental Material Date: 2026-05-18 06:08 Link: https://edstem.org/us/courses/98593/discussion/8077944 Pinned: False | Votes: 12 | Views: 4030 Post This post is a brief discussion on math notation and the nature of recurrence relations. See post #9 which details the solution format for Dynamic Programming. ---- Table of Contents ----- Context What is a Recurrence Relation? Examples FAQ ---- 1. Context: Part 2, or the "recurrence relation", in a DP problem solution for this course is providing a mathematical definition of the table entries for the solution. In contrast, Part 1, or the "subproblem definition", is providing a short explanation in English explaining the meaning of each table entry, and Part 3, or the "Implementation Analysis", is an assessment of building and using this table defined in the "recurrence relation" to solve the original problem. Think of it this way: the recurrence relation is the mathematical specification, and implementation would be the step by step algorithm or code that implements it. 2. What is a Recurrence Relation? Dynamic Programming uses recurrence relations. Without a recurrence relation, a solution is not a Dynamic Programming solution. Key characteristics of a recurrence relation: A recurrence relation is an equation. The equation provides a general rule for the recurrence. The rule defines the next term as a function of the previous term(s). To generate the sequence (or table) using the general rule, your general rule may depend on values being pre-defined for some early elements in this sequence, which are called base cases. Typically, separate equations are used for the base case(s). For example, here is a recurrence relation and base cases for LCS (DP1 lecture 24+): Base cases: L(i,0) = 0, 0 <= i <= n L(0,j) = 0, 1 <= j <= n Recurrence: L(i,j) = max{L(i-1,j), L(i,j-1) }, if x_i != y_j = 1 + L(i-1, j-1), if x_i = y_j where i,j: 1 <= i <= n, 1 <= j <= n Note: There are multiple base cases, and the bounds for the indices i and j are specified. If no indices are used (such as when there are only one or two individual base case table entries), bounds are not required. For example, T[1] = 1 does not have bounds because no variable is used. The bounds for the indices in the base cases are different and specified separately from the bounds for recurrence relation. Frequently, the bounds will be slightly different between the base cases and the recurrence relation. The bounds as provided convey directionality of your table fill. That is, when the range is 0 <= i <= n, we assume a fill order of left to right. If your approach depends on a right to left fill order (common for suffix-based approaches), then your bounds need to reflect this as well, such as n >= i >= 0. Each unique table entry cannot be contradicted by setting it multiple times. In math, you cannot say "x=5" and "x=3" simultaneously. This should be reflected by comprehensive and mutually-exclusive conditions where otherwise can be used to specify "all other conditions". Similarly, a table entry defined mathematically can only have one value. For example, in this example definition, L(0,7) is defined under the base cases, and L(1, 5) would be defined by one of the two options in the recurrence relation. Exception: it is ok for the exact same table entry to be specified more than once in a recurrence relation, as long as it is set equal to the same value across all instances. Table entries cannot be overwritten. While this would be acceptable in pseudocode where there is a sense of "before", "after", and "iterations", these concepts are not present in a mathematical definition. A table entry is not a temporary variable to store data in this context; it is a clearly, mathematically defined value. This point is similar to the previous bullet. All table entries that are used to generate the value of any other table entry have a clearly defined value. In other words, there is no scenario where an undefined table entry is used to calculate the value of another table entry. 3. Examples Example 1: Example 1: Incorrect version 1 Base Case: T[i] = 0, 0 <= i <= n Recurrence: T[i] = T[i-1]+1, 1 <= i <=n What's wrong: Multiple assignments to the same elements. All the entries defined by the recurrence relation are overlapping with the base cases. Example 1: Incorrect version 2 Base Case: T[0] = 0 Recurrence: if i >= 1 T[i] = T[i-1]+1 What's wrong: Incomplete bounds for i in the recurrence relation (needs to have a ceiling because our tables are finite in size), and use of an if statement construct (which is a pseudocode construct, and not a math construct). Example 1: Incorrect Version 3 Base Case: for i = 0->n: T[i] = 0 Recurrence: for i = 1->n: T[i] = T[i-1]+1 What's wrong: This is completely pseudocode. for loop constructs are used, and the table entries are defined more than once. This is not a mathematical definition for table entries. Example 1 (corrected): Base Case: T[0] = 0 Recurrence: T[i] = T[i-1]+1, 1 <= i <= n Example 2: Example 2: Incorrect version Base Case: T[0] = 0 Recurrence: T[i] = max{T[i],T[i-1]+1}, 1 <= i <= n What's wrong: T[i] is on both the left and the right sides of the equation. The undefined T[i] is being used to define T[i]. With this incorrect version, it looks like there is an attempt to use pseudocode, but the mathematical definition for a recurrence relation is an equation and not a step-by-step procedure. Each table entry can only be defined once, and there is no such thing as "prior value." Example 2 (corrected): Base Case: T[0] = 0 Recurrence: T[i] = max{T[j]+1}, 1 <= i <= n and 0 <= j < i You can add helper indices if needed to access a range of previous table entries, as long as the helper indices are clearly defined (bounded). Other Notes: Some ways you can write a range: T[i] = 0, 0 <= i <= n T[i] = 0 where 0 <= i <= n T[i] = 0 s.t. 0 <= i <= n 4. FAQ: Q: Should table entries use [] or ()? A: Either is fine, just be consistent. Q: Would you define an empty set/list/array as {}? A: Yes, that's the standard notation. It can also be shown as the greek Phi ϕ, but do not use special symbols in Canvas submissions due to issues with porting format to the grading environment. Q: Can a base case be a non-discrete value, such as T[i,0] = T[i-1, 0]+1, 1<=i<=n as long as it's not the same as the recurrence function? A: We are expecting base cases to be a terminating scenario that does not use recursion to produce an answer, such as a hard-coded value for those earlier elements of the table. Q: Are we allowed to use "or", "and", "||", "&&", logical not (!x), "infinity" in our recurrence definition? A: Yes. 5: Q: When is it ok to use "if" and not ok to use "if"? A: You can use "if" for conditions (frequently on the right side of a multiline equation) to specify which right-hand side to use for an equation when defining a value. You cannot use an if statement (control of flow construct). Q

title: Dynamic Programming Guidance
text: Dynamic Programming Guidance Category: Staff Use Only > Supplemental Material Date: 2026-05-18 06:07 Link: https://edstem.org/us/courses/98593/discussion/8077940 Pinned: False | Votes: 12 | Views: 4939 Post Solutions for Dynamic Programming (DP) Homework are in a written format with required components. This post is intended to give a general overview of those parts of such a solution. You will also see quiz and exam questions based on what constitutes a complete solution for DP. General Rules Submissions must be a Dynamic Programming approach to be eligible for points. No recursion or memoization. Inputs are always indexed starting from 1, unless stated otherwise (i.e. A[0] is usually invalid). Inputs may not be modified - they are to be treated as immutable. Tables may start from anywhere. If you need T[0] or T[-1] for a base case, this is valid as long as it is defined. Tables may only store primitive values: integers, decimals, characters, or Booleans. Required Sections (a) Subproblem Definition ALWAYS build on a subset of the input (e.g., A[1..i]). Conceptually, the subproblem should always be defined against "previous" subproblems and not on the entire input. Is represented using words: "T(i,j) is the ....." Should use words from the problem statement without redefining their meaning May or may not require inclusion of the last element: ", including A[i]" The lectures use the phrase "which includes a[i]" to add the constraint "element a[i] must be used in the subproblem output." The phrases "which includes i" and "ending at a[i]" would also imply this constraint. These are not referring to the range of indices considered for the subproblem, 1,2,...,i, which is assumed inclusive [1, i]. (b) Recurrence Definition #10 #10 #10 Read, absorb, and love #10 #10 #10 Expressed using mathematical notation - no programming constructs and not in words The table gets a single mathematical definition for all entries - there is no logic/flow control structure other than stating a set of conditional definitions. Defines the value of all table entries recursively to smaller subproblems Any referenced table entry must also be well-defined i.e. a reference for T(i-2) for 1 <= i <= n requires a definition for T(-1) It cannot be defined self-referentially (e.g. T(i) = max(T(i), ...)) When base case(s) are necessary, include them and their applicable bounds Example: T(i,0) = 0 for 1 <= i <= n Always provide bounds for any variables used, scoped to where they are used Focus on table definitions, not a final solution (i.e. no return value) (c) Implementation Analysis (1,2,3,4) These parts should be based on the recurrence you use in the previous section. (1) State the number of subproblems in big-O notation This scales similar to runtime complexity A one-dimensional table ranging from indices -1 to n would be O(n). e.g. O(n), O(n*m) (2) State the runtime to fill this table Always, always in this course - worst case Big-O notation. e.g. O(n^2) (3) State how/where the final return is extracted from that table e.g. sum{T(*)}, max{T(*)}, T(n), max(T(n), T(n-1)) (4) State the runtime to extract the final return in big-O notation Tips and Tricks You will be tempted to start with code. This is a terrible idea. Starting with code ensures your recurrence will be nearly impossible to craft. Do these sections in order - Dr. Vigoda does the same thing, and you should see how seamlessly the final solution comes together. Define a subproblem, which can then be translated to the mathematical recurrence, and the analysis can be easily derived from that. The lectures contain multiple approaches (LIS, LCS, Knapsack, CMM, etc.). When solving a new problem, first try to think of which approach makes the most sense to adapt for a solution to the new problem. Example Solution Expand the spoiler below for an example of what a complete DP solution looks like: Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) a. Define the entries of your table in words. E.g., T(i) or T(i, j) is ... Let T(i) = length of LIS in a[1...i] which includes a[i] b. State recurrence for entries of your table in terms of smaller subproblems. T(1) = 1 T(i) = 1 + max{T(j) if a[i] > a[j] : where 1 <= j <= i-1} where 2 <= i <= n c. Implementation Analysis (1) State the number of subproblems: O(n) (2) State the runtime for table fill: O(n^2) (3) State how the return is extracted: return max{T(*)} (4) State the runtime of that return extraction: O(n) Please use this thread to ask any follow-up questions regarding what constitutes a complete DP solution, or clarifications on the above information. title: Graph Theory Guidance
text: Graph Theory Guidance Category: Staff Use Only > Supplemental Material Date: 2026-06-15 17:47 Link: https://edstem.org/us/courses/98593/discussion/8129087 Pinned: True | Votes: 3 | Views: 1437 Post Below you will find information on how you should approach solving Graph Theory problems in this class. Feel free to ask questions about writing a Graph Theory solution in this thread. Context A graph theory problem is a form of reduction. You are given some problem to solve, and the challenge is to solve that problem by using one of the graph algorithms we study this semester. In other words, you reduce your problem to a graph problem such that a known algorithm can solve it for you. The known algorithms (#192) are treated as a black-box - you are not allowed to change / modify them in any way. You can (and often will) modify the original problem input in some way or create a graph which represents the input - this becomes the input to the black-box. You then use the outputs from the black-box (possibly with modification) to output a solution to the original problem. Rules NO PSEUDOCODE - YOU WILL LOSE A LOT OF POINTS You may only use known black box algorithms (#192). You must use one or more black box algorithms (#192) to solve problems. You must not modify any black box algorithms (#192). Re-creating a black box with modifications is the same thing. All graphs are in adjacency list format (see below) You can assume you always have n and m for purposes of runtime analysis, where n represents the number of vertices |V| and m represents the number of edges |E|. #192 isn't exactly relevant here, but it's worth another link. See #8 for common course runtimes, including operations performed to graphs. Required Parts of a Graph Theory Solution (a) Algorithm Describe "how" to solve the problem in words (narrative/paragraph form) Bulleted steps are fine, but pseudocode/code is not allowed "Pseudocode" includes line-by-line restatement of code as words. If you find yourself using a lot of nested bullets, you might be erring on the side of line-by-line conversion of code into words, which is not a narrative. Describe what modifications are performed to the input, if any Detail the black box algorithm(s) being used What is passed in as input What is being used from its output Describe what modifications are performed to the output, if any Repeat as needed for any other black boxes Detail the final return for the problem's solution (b) Justification of Correctness Describe "why" your algorithm solves the problem, in words (narrative). This is not a formal inductive proof; informal justification is ok. Explain how your problem is solved by the black boxes used Explain how changing the inputs and/or outputs gets what you want (c) Runtime Analysis Analyze all steps of the algorithm in Big-O notation. Always, always in this course - worst case Big-O. Can explain/analyze in words and assert known black-box runtimes without further justification. Must include run time analysis for any pre/post processing See #8 for common runtimes when dealing with graphs. Provide an overall final runtime in (you guessed it) Big-O notation, fully simplified. If the problem specifies a target runtime, you must meet that for full credit. If we do not specify a runtime, faster (and correct) in Big O notation are worth more credit. While we work with worse-case runtimes, we also work with tightest-bound runtimes. That is, even though the worst case for an O(n+m) operation occurs when m= n^2, it is incorrect to state O(n^2) for such an operation unless we know something about the graph (e.g., it is a clique). O(n+m) is the tightest worse-case bound on such an operation Similarly, if we know the graph is a tree, then m = n-1 and O(n+m) operations collapse to O(n). When conveying the well-known runtimes of usable black boxes, you are not required to simplify it, even if the graph's constraints would allow for it. Graph Representation Graphs in this class, unless stated otherwise: Are simple graphs (no multi-edges or self-loops) Are always given in adjacency list format (see DPV 3.1.1) An array of vertices that are pointers to linked lists of adjacent vertices, such that each element in the list represents an edge to another vertex. Vertices are assumed to be labeled in such a way where they can be found by indexing into the array. Can be connected or disconnected, undirected or directed, sparse or dense If unstated by the problem, you should assume any of these could be true Are connected and undirected when working with MSTs Are connected and directed when working with flow networks When working with graphs, there are a number of common actions that can be presented without needing to go into details. Some examples as follows: Reversing a graph: "Reverse the edges in the graph". Copying a graph: "Create a copy of G and call it GCopy". Creating subgraphs: "Create a copy of G called GEven that only keeps edges with even numbered weights". "Create a copy of G called GK that only keeps vertices with a degree > k". "Create a residual graph of G called Grs that uses the flows from our run of EK". Unless the task can't directly be completed in this runtime, each of these are assumed to be O(n + m) operations. Please ask questions regarding the expectations for Graph Theory problems in this thread. Examples for 3.16 (minimum number of semesters necessary to complete the curriculum) Run topological sort on the graph. title: 📋 Graphs Module — Master Study Plan
text:
📋 Graphs Module — Master Study Plan Prerequisites Checklist Before diving in, make sure you're comfortable with: Basic set notation (unions, intersections, complements) Big-O notation and analyzing loops Recursion and recursive thinking Basic proof techniques (contradiction, induction) Matrix multiplication (for GR4) Basic probability (for GR4) What a tree is (connected, acyclic graph) If any of these feel shaky, spend 30 minutes reviewing before starting Day 1. 10-Day Study Plan (~2 hours/day) Day 1: Graph Fundamentals — Representations & Traversals 📖 Read: 01-GR0-Graph-Fundamentals.md (Sections 1–4: Terminology through BFS) 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 3.1–3.2 Focus: Terminology: vertices, edges, directed vs undirected, degree Adjacency list vs adjacency matrix — know the tradeoffs BFS algorithm and its O(n+m) runtime Dijkstra's as "BFS with weights" ✅ Checkpoint: Can you trace BFS on a 6-node graph and produce dist/prev arrays? Day 2: DFS Deep Dive 📖 Read: 01-GR0-Graph-Fundamentals.md (Sections 5–8: DFS through Edge Types) 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 3.2–3.3 Focus: DFS for undirected graphs (connected components) DFS for directed graphs (pre/post numbering) The four edge types: tree, back, forward, cross Key fact: G has a cycle ⟺ DFS tree has a back edge ✅ Checkpoint: Given a directed graph, can you run DFS and label every edge by type? Day 3: Topological Sort + SCC Setup 📖 Read: 01-GR0-Graph-Fundamentals.md (Section 9: Topological Sort) + 02-GR1-Strongly-Connected-Components.md (Sections 1–3) 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 3.3–3.4 Focus: Topological sorting via DFS (sort by decreasing post number) Only works on DAGs — why? Definition of strongly connected components The metagraph is always a DAG ✅ Checkpoint: Can you topologically sort a DAG by hand using post numbers? Day 4: SCC Algorithm — The Main Event 📖 Read: 02-GR1-Strongly-Connected-Components.md (Sections 4–7) 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 3.4 Focus: The two-pass DFS algorithm for finding SCCs Why we reverse the graph (swap sources/sinks) The key lemma: highest post-number vertex is in a source SCC Full worked example ✅ Checkpoint: Can you find all SCCs of a 10-node directed graph by hand? Can you explain why the algorithm works? Day 5: 2-SAT 📖 Read: 03-GR2-2SAT.md 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 3.4 (applications) Focus: CNF notation, k-SAT definition Constructing the implication graph from 2-SAT clauses The lemma: satisfiable ⟺ no x_i and ¬x_i in same SCC The full algorithm: build graph → find SCCs → assign truth values ✅ Checkpoint: Given a 2-SAT formula with 4 variables, can you construct the implication graph, find SCCs, and determine satisfiability? Day 6: MST — Kruskal's & The Cut Property 📖 Read: 04-GR3-Minimum-Spanning-Tree.md (Sections 1–5) 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 5.1 Focus: MST definition and tree properties Kruskal's algorithm with Union-Find The Cut Property (statement AND proof) Why Kruskal's is correct (via Cut Property) ✅ Checkpoint: Can you state and prove the Cut Property? Can you trace Kruskal's on a weighted graph? Day 7: MST — Prim's + Review 📖 Read: 04-GR3-Minimum-Spanning-Tree.md (Sections 6–7) 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 5.1 Focus: Prim's algorithm and its connection to Cut Property Kruskal's vs Prim's comparison Review all MST material ✅ Checkpoint: Can you explain how both Kruskal's and Prim's are special cases of the Cut Property? Day 8: Markov Chains 📖 Read: 05-GR4-Markov-Chains-PageRank.md (Sections 1–5: through Ergodic) Focus: Markov chain definition, transition matrices k-step transitions via matrix powers Stationary distribution: πP = π Ergodic = aperiodic + irreducible (strongly connected) ✅ Checkpoint: Given a 3-state Markov chain, can you write the transition matrix and compute P²? Day 9: PageRank 📖 Read: 05-GR4-Markov-Chains-PageRank.md (Sections 6–9: PageRank onward) Focus: Citation count intuition → random surfer model The damping parameter α and why it's needed Handling sink nodes The transition matrix for PageRank Finding π in practice ✅ Checkpoint: Can you explain why the random surfer model guarantees ergodicity? What happens with sink nodes? Day 10: Comprehensive Review 📖 Read: 06-Graphs-Cheat-Sheet.md — use this as your self-test Activities: Work through the cheat sheet: cover each algorithm's entry, try to recall it before reading Do 2–3 practice problems from each module Re-read any sections where you got stuck Write your own 1-page summary from memory ✅ Final Checkpoint: Can you explain every algorithm's input/output/runtime without looking? Can you prove the Cut Property and the 2-SAT lemma? Module Dependency Graph graph TD GR0[GR0: Graph Fundamentals
BFS, DFS, Topo Sort] --> GR1[GR1: SCCs
2-pass DFS] GR1 --> GR2[GR2: 2-SAT
Implication Graphs] GR0 --> GR3[GR3: MST
Kruskal's, Prim's] GR1 --> GR4[GR4: Markov Chains
& PageRank] Key dependency: GR1 (SCCs) is the linchpin — it's needed for both 2-SAT and PageRank. Practice Problem Sources Module Suggested Practice GR0 DPV Ch 3, Exercises 3.1–3.5 GR1 DPV Ch 3, Exercises 3.10–3.14 GR2 DPV Ch 3, Exercise 3.28 (2-SAT) GR3 DPV Ch 5, Exercises 5.1–5.9 GR4 Past exam problems on Markov chains Also check the homework/ and ed-discussions/ folders for course-specific practice.

title: GR0: Graph Fundamentals
text:
GR0: Graph Fundamentals 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 3 (Decompositions of Graphs) This is the foundation for everything. If you struggled with graphs before, this is where we fix that. 1. What Is a Graph? A graph G = (V, E) consists of: V — a set of vertices (aka nodes) E — a set of edges connecting pairs of vertices graph LR A --- B A --- C B --- C Two flavors: Undirected: edges are unordered pairs {u, v}. If A connects to B, then B connects to A. Directed: edges are ordered pairs (u, v). A → B does NOT mean B → A. graph LR A -->|directed| B A -->|directed| C B -->|directed| C We assume all graphs are simple — no self-loops (u, u) and no parallel edges. 2. Terminology Term Definition Adjacent Two vertices sharing an edge Neighborhood N(v) Set of vertices adjacent to v Degree deg(v) Number of edges incident to v; equals |N(v)| In-degree (directed) Number of edges coming into v Out-degree (directed) Number of edges going out of v Path Sequence of vertices connected by edges Cycle Path that starts and ends at the same vertex Connected (undirected) There's a path between every pair of vertices Strongly connected (directed) For every u, v: path from u→v AND v→u DAG Directed Acyclic Graph — directed graph with no cycles Source Vertex with in-degree 0 (nothing points to it) Sink Vertex with out-degree 0 (it points to nothing) ⚠️ Common Pitfall: In directed graphs, adjacency has direction. "A is adjacent to B" means there's an edge A → B. It does NOT mean B → A. 3. Graph Representations Adjacency Matrix An n × n matrix M where M[i][j] = 1 if edge (i, j) exists, 0 otherwise. Example (undirected): Example (directed): 1 2 3 1 2 3 1 [ 0 1 1 ] 1 [ 0 1 1 ] 2 [ 1 0 1 ] 2 [ 0 0 1 ] 3 [ 1 1 0 ] 3 [ 0 1 0 ] Space: O(n²) Check if edge exists: O(1) ✅ List all neighbors: O(n) ❌ Undirected → symmetric matrix Adjacency List Each vertex stores a list of its neighbors. Example (directed): 1 → [2, 3] 2 → [3] 3 → [2] Space: O(n + m) where m = |E| Check if edge exists: O(degree) ❌ List all neighbors: O(degree) ✅ 💡 Intuition: For sparse graphs (m ≪ n²), adjacency lists win. Most real-world graphs are sparse. This course uses adjacency lists unless stated otherwise. Adjacency Matrix Adjacency List Space O(n²) O(n + m) Edge lookup O(1) O(degree) Iterate neighbors O(n) O(degree) Best for Dense graphs Sparse graphs (most cases) 4. BFS: Breadth-First Search BFS explores the graph level by level, like ripples in a pond. It finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs. BFS(G, s): Input: G = (V, E), source vertex s Output: dist[v] = min edges from s to v prev[v] = predecessor on shortest path for all v ∈ V: dist[v] = ∞ prev[v] = null dist[s] = 0 Q = queue containing s while Q is not empty: v = dequeue(Q) for all edges (v, w) ∈ E: if dist[w] = ∞: // not yet visited dist[w] = dist[v] + 1 prev[w] = v enqueue(Q, w) Runtime: O(n + m) — each vertex and edge visited once. BFS Example graph LR A((A
d=0)) --> B((B
d=1)) A --> C((C
d=1)) B --> D((D
d=2)) C --> D C --> E((E
d=2)) Starting from A: BFS visits {A} → {B, C} → {D, E} 🎯 Exam Tip: BFS gives shortest paths in unweighted graphs only. For weighted graphs, you need Dijkstra's. 5. Dijkstra's Algorithm Dijkstra's is BFS for weighted graphs (with positive weights only). Dijkstra(G, s, w): Input: G = (V, E), source s, weights w(e) > 0 for all e Output: dist[v] = shortest weighted path from s to v prev[v] = predecessor on shortest path for all v ∈ V: dist[v] = ∞ prev[v] = null dist[s] = 0 H = min-heap with all vertices, keyed by dist while H is not empty: v = extractMin(H) for all edges (v, w) ∈ E: if dist[v] + w(v,w) < dist[w]: dist[w] = dist[v] + w(v,w) prev[w] = v decreaseKey(H, w) Runtime: O((|V| + |E|) log |V|) using a binary min-heap. Key lemmas: The subpath of any shortest path is itself a shortest path Triangle inequality: dist(u, v) ≤ dist(u, x) + dist(x, v) ⚠️ Common Pitfall: Dijkstra's does NOT work with negative edge weights. For negative weights, use Bellman-Ford (covered in DP3). 6. DFS: Depth-First Search (Undirected) DFS explores as deep as possible before backtracking. It's the foundation for SCCs, topological sort, and cycle detection. DFS for Finding Connected Components DFS(G): Input: G = (V, E) undirected, adjacency list Output: ccnum[v] = connected component number for each v cc = 0 for all v ∈ V: visited[v] = false for all v ∈ V: if not visited[v]: cc = cc + 1 Explore(v) Explore(z): visited[z] = true ccnum[z] = cc for all (z, w) ∈ E: if not visited[w]: Explore(w) Runtime: O(n + m) 💡 Intuition: Each call to Explore from the main DFS loop discovers a new connected component. Everything reachable from z gets the same cc number. graph TD subgraph "Component 1" A --- B B --- C end subgraph "Component 2" D --- E end subgraph "Component 3" F end 7. DFS for Directed Graphs — Pre/Post Numbering For directed graphs, we add a clock to track the order of discovery (pre) and completion (post) for each vertex. DFS(G): Input: G = (V, E) directed, adjacency list Output: pre[v], post[v] for each vertex clock = 1 for all v ∈ V: visited[v] = false for all v ∈ V: if not visited[v]: Explore(v) Explore(z): pre[z] = clock; clock++ visited[z] = true for all (z, w) ∈ E: if not visited[w]: Explore(w) post[z] = clock; clock++ Runtime: O(n + m) Worked Example graph LR A --> B A --> C B --> D B --> E D --> E E --> A Running DFS starting from A: Vertex pre post A 1 10 B 2 9 D 3 6 E 4 5 C 7 8 The pre/post intervals tell us about the tree structure: Containment: If [pre(u), post(u)] contains [pre(v), post(v)], then u is an ancestor of v Disjoint: If intervals don't overlap, neither is an ancestor of the other 8. Edge Types in DFS When we run DFS on a directed graph, every edge falls into exactly one of four categories: Edge Type Relationship Post Numbers Tree edge Parent → child in DFS tree post(z) > post(w) Back edge Descendant → ancestor post(z) < post(w) ⚠️ Forward edge Ancestor → non-child descendant post(z) > post(w) Cross edge Between unrelated vertices post(z) > post(w) graph TD A -->|tree| B A -->|tree| C B -->|tree| D D -->|back| A A -->|forward| D C -->|cross| D ⚠️ Common Pitfall: Back edges are the ONLY type where post(z) < post(w). For all other edges, post(z) > post(w). This is critical for understanding cycles and topological sort. The Cycle Theorem G has a cycle ⟺ its DFS tree has a back edge. Proof (→): Consider a cycle C. Let v_i be the first vertex of C visited by DFS. All other cycle vertices are reachable from v_i, so they become descendants. The edge v_{i-1} → v_i goes from descendant to ancestor = back edge. ✅ Proof (←): A back edge v → w means v is a descendant of w. So there's a path w ↝ v in the tree, plus the edge v → w. That's a cycle. ✅ 9. Topological Sorting A topological ordering of a DAG is a linear ordering of vertices such that for every edge u → v, u comes before v. 💡 Intuition: Think of it as a valid scheduling order. If task A must happen before task B, then A appears first. The Algorithm: Run DFS, then sort vertices by dec

title: GR1: Strongly Connected Components
text: GR1: Strongly Connected Components 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 3.4 SCCs are the directed-graph analog of connected components. The algorithm is elegant: just two passes of DFS. 1. Definition Strongly Connected: Vertices v and w are strongly connected if there's a path v ↝ w AND a path w ↝ v. Strongly Connected Component (SCC): A maximal set of mutually strongly connected vertices. "Maximal" means you can't add any more vertices and still have everyone strongly connected. graph LR subgraph SCC1["SCC: A,B,E"] A --> B B --> E E --> A end subgraph SCC2["SCC: C,D"] C --> D D --> C end subgraph SCC3["SCC: F"] F end B --> C D --> F E --> F This graph has 3 SCCs: {A, B, E}, {C, D}, and {F}. 2. The Metagraph — Every Directed Graph Is a DAG of Its SCCs If you collapse each SCC into a single "meta-vertex" and keep the edges between them, you get a DAG. graph LR S1["SCC: A,B,E"] --> S2["SCC: C,D"] S1 --> S3["SCC: F"] S2 --> S3 Key Property: The metagraph of SCCs is always a DAG. Why? If there were a cycle between two SCCs S and S', then every vertex in S could reach every vertex in S' and vice versa — meaning they should be merged into one bigger SCC. Contradiction. 3. The Goal We want to find all SCCs of a directed graph in O(n + m) time. Strategy: Think of the metagraph as a DAG. If we can identify the SCCs one at a time (say, starting from sinks), we can peel them off. Key insight: If we pick any vertex v in a sink SCC and run Explore(v), we visit exactly the vertices in that SCC (and nothing else — because a sink SCC has no outgoing edges in the metagraph). So the plan is: Find a vertex in a sink SCC Explore from it → discover the entire sink SCC Remove it Repeat 4. Finding a Sink SCC via the Reverse Graph Problem: How do we find a vertex guaranteed to be in a sink SCC? 💡 Intuition: We can't directly find a sink SCC, but we CAN find a source SCC — it's the one containing the vertex with the highest post number after DFS. And here's the trick: sources in G^R are sinks in G (and vice versa). G^R is the reverse graph — same vertices, all edges flipped: E^R = {(w, v) : (v, w) ∈ E} The SCCs are the same in G and G^R (reversing edges doesn't change strong connectivity). But sources and sinks swap! The Key Lemma Lemma: The vertex with the highest post number (from DFS on G^R) lies in a source SCC of G^R, which is a sink SCC of G. Proof sketch: For SCCs S and S' with an edge S → S' in the metagraph, the maximum post number in S is greater than the max post number in S'. The SCC with the globally highest post number must therefore be a source (nothing has a higher post number pointing into it). In G^R, this source corresponds to a sink in G. 5. The SCC Algorithm SCC(G): Input: Directed graph G = (V, E) Output: ccnum[v] = SCC number for each vertex 1. Construct G^R (reverse all edges) 2. Run DFS(G^R) — compute post numbers 3. Order vertices by decreasing post number 4. Run DFS(G) using this vertex ordering — each call to Explore from the main loop discovers a new SCC Runtime: O(n + m) Step 1: O(n + m) to construct reverse graph Step 2: O(n + m) for DFS Step 3: O(n) for ordering (or just process in decreasing order) Step 4: O(n + m) for DFS Why Step 4 Works In Step 4, we process vertices in decreasing post-number order from G^R. The first vertex we pick is in a sink SCC of G. Explore visits exactly that SCC (it can't escape — no outgoing metagraph edges). We mark it and continue. The next unvisited vertex with the highest remaining post number is in the next sink SCC (after removing the previous one). And so on. 6. Full Worked Example Consider this directed graph G: graph LR A --> B B --> C C --> A B --> D D --> E E --> D C --> E Step 1: Construct G^R (reverse all edges) graph LR B --> A C --> B A --> C D --> B E --> D D --> E E --> C Step 2: Run DFS on G^R (starting from, say, A) Vertex pre post A 1 10 C 2 9 B 3 8 D 4 7 E 5 6 Step 3: Order by decreasing post number A (10), C (9), B (8), D (7), E (6) Step 4: Run DFS on G (original) in this order Start with A → Explore finds: A → B → C → (A already visited) → D → E → (D already visited). Wait — this visits everything! Hmm, let me redo this more carefully. Let's use a different example where SCCs are clearer. Better Example graph LR A --> B B --> A B --> C C --> D D --> C D --> E SCCs: {A, B}, {C, D}, {E} Step 1: G^R: graph LR B --> A A --> B C --> B D --> C C --> D E --> D Step 2: DFS on G^R (start from A): Explore A → B → (A visited) → C → D → (C visited) → E → done backtracking. Vertex pre post A 1 10 B 2 9 C 3 8 D 4 7 E 5 6 Step 3: Order by decreasing post: A, B, C, D, E Step 4: DFS on G in this order: Explore(A): visits A → B → (A visited) → C → D → (C visited) → E Oops, this is because {A,B} can reach {C,D} can reach {E} OK — the trick is that the DFS in step 4 uses the undirected-style DFS (with component labeling). Each Explore call from the outer loop labels a new SCC. But within Explore, we only follow edges in G. Actually, let me reconsider. The key is that Step 4's DFS processes vertices in the order from Step 3. Since A has the highest post number in G^R, it's in a source SCC of G^R = sink SCC of G. But {A,B} is NOT a sink SCC here... Let me recheck. In G: {A,B} → {C,D} → {E}. So {E} is the sink SCC, {A,B} is the source. In G^R: edges are reversed, so {E} → {C,D} → {A,B}. So {A,B} is the sink SCC of G^R = source SCC of G^R... wait. The algorithm says: run DFS on G^R to find vertex ordering, then run DFS on G. The vertex with the highest post in G^R is in a source SCC of G^R = source SCC of G^R. But we want to find sink SCCs of G = source SCCs of G^R. ✅ So in G^R, vertex A has post=10 (highest). A is in a source SCC of G^R. Source of G^R = Sink of G... but {A,B} is NOT the sink of G! Let me reconsider: In G^R: {A,B} has an incoming edge from C (C→B in G^R). So {A,B} is NOT a source in G^R. {E} has an outgoing edge E→D in G^R, and incoming? Nothing points to E in G^R. So {E} is a source in G^R. But E has post=6, the LOWEST. I think the issue is my DFS traversal on G^R. Let me redo it starting from E: DFS on G^R starting from E: Explore(E): E → D → C → B → A → (B visited, back to A) → (back up) ... Starting vertex choice matters! DFS iterates over ALL vertices. Let's say the outer loop goes A, B, C, D, E: Explore(A): A → B (in G^R, edges from A are: A→C via C→A reversed? No: G has A→B, B→A, B→C, C→D, D→C, D→E. So G^R has: B→A, A→B, C→B, D→C, C→D, E→D. G^R adjacency list: A: [B] (from B→A in G^R; because A→B in G) B: [A] (from A→B in G^R; because B→A in G) C: [B, D] (from B→C reversed gives C→B; D→C reversed gives C→D) D: [C, E] (from C→D reversed gives D→C; but wait... C→D in G means D→C in G^R. And D→E in G means E→D in G^R) Let me redo: G edges: A→B, B→A, B→C, C→D, D→C, D→E G^R edges: B→A, A→B, C→B, D→C, C→D, E→D G^R adjacency list: A: [B] B: [A] C: [B, D] D: [C] E: [D] DFS on G^R, outer loop order A, B, C, D, E: Explore(A): visit A, go to B. Visit B, go to A (visited). Backtrack. post(B)=2, post(A)=3. B already visited. Skip. Ex

title: GR2: 2-Satisfiability (2-SAT)
text: GR2: 2-Satisfiability (2-SAT) 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 3.4 (Applications of SCC) This is a beautiful application of SCCs. We reduce a logic problem to a graph problem and solve it in linear time. 1. SAT and CNF — The Setup Boolean Formulas We work with boolean variables x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ, each either TRUE (1) or FALSE (0). For each variable xᵢ, we have two literals: xᵢ and x̄ᵢ (its negation/complement). Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) A formula in CNF is an AND of OR-clauses: f = (x2) ∧ (x̄3 ∨ x4) ∧ (x3 ∨ x̄5 ∨ x̄1 ∨ x2) Clause: An OR (∨) of literals, e.g., (x₃ ∨ x̄₅ ∨ x̄₁) Formula: An AND (∧) of clauses Satisfying assignment: Values for each variable that make the entire formula TRUE This means at least one literal in every clause must be TRUE The SAT Problem Input: A formula f in CNF with n variables and m clauses Output: A satisfying assignment, or "NO" if none exists 2. k-SAT k-SAT restricts every clause to have at most k literals. k Complexity 1 Trivial (each clause forces a variable) 2 Polynomial — O(n + m) via SCCs! 3+ NP-complete (no known polynomial algorithm) The jump from k=2 to k=3 is one of the most important complexity boundaries in CS. We're going to solve 2-SAT. 3. The Implication Graph Here's the key idea: every 2-SAT clause can be rewritten as two implications. For a clause (α ∨ β): If α is FALSE, then β MUST be TRUE → implication: ᾱ → β If β is FALSE, then α MUST be TRUE → implication: β̄ → α Construction Given a 2-SAT formula with n variables and m clauses: Vertices: 2n vertices — one for each literal xᵢ and x̄ᵢ Edges: 2m directed edges — two implications per clause Clause Edge 1 Edge 2 (α ∨ β) ᾱ → β β̄ → α Example f = (x̄1 ∨ x̄2) ∧ (x2 ∨ x3) ∧ (x̄3 ∨ x̄1) Clause Implications Edges (x̄₁ ∨ x̄₂) x₁ → x̄₂, x₂ → x̄₁ x₁ → x̄₂, x₂ → x̄₁ (x₂ ∨ x₃) x̄₂ → x₃, x̄₃ → x₂ x̄₂ → x₃, x̄₃ → x₂ (x̄₃ ∨ x̄₁) x₃ → x̄₁, x₁ → x̄₃ x₃ → x̄₁, x₁ → x̄₃ graph LR x1["x1"] --> nx2["not-x2"] x2["x2"] --> nx1["not-x1"] nx2 --> x3["x3"] nx3["not-x3"] --> x2 x3 --> nx1 x1 --> nx3 💡 Intuition: Paths in this graph are chains of logical implications. If there's a path x₁ ↝ x̄₁, it means "if x₁ is TRUE, then x₁ must be FALSE" — a contradiction (unless x₁ is just FALSE). 4. The Key Lemma Lemma: A 2-SAT formula f is satisfiable if and only if for all i, xᵢ and x̄ᵢ are in different SCCs of the implication graph. Equivalently: f is unsatisfiable ⟺ there exists some i where xᵢ and x̄ᵢ are in the same SCC. Why "same SCC = unsatisfiable" If xᵢ and x̄ᵢ are in the same SCC, then: There's a path xᵢ ↝ x̄ᵢ (if xᵢ is TRUE, then x̄ᵢ must be TRUE → contradiction) There's a path x̄ᵢ ↝ xᵢ (if x̄ᵢ is TRUE, then xᵢ must be TRUE → same contradiction) Neither assignment works → unsatisfiable Why "different SCCs = satisfiable" This is the harder direction. We need to actually construct a satisfying assignment. That's where the algorithm comes in. 5. The 2-SAT Algorithm 2SAT(f): Input: Formula f in 2-CNF with n variables, m clauses Output: Satisfying assignment or "unsatisfiable" 1. Construct the implication graph G (2n vertices, 2m edges) 2. Run SCC algorithm on G → get SCCs in reverse topological order (sink first) 3. For each i: if xᵢ and x̄ᵢ are in the same SCC, return "unsatisfiable" 4. For each SCC S (in reverse topological order, sink → source): - If literals in S are unassigned: - Set all literals in S to TRUE - Set all complement literals (in S̄) to FALSE 5. Return assignment Runtime: O(n + m) — dominated by the SCC computation. 6. Why the Algorithm Is Correct The Assignment Strategy 💡 Intuition: Sink SCCs are "safe" to set TRUE because they have no outgoing implications to worry about. Their complements are source SCCs (safe to set FALSE because FALSE can imply anything). Claim: If S is a sink SCC, then S̄ is a source SCC Proof: First, we need a helper fact: Fact: If there's a path α ↝ β in the implication graph, then there's a path β̄ ↝ ᾱ. Why? Each edge ᾱ → β in the implication graph came from a clause (α ∨ β), which also produced the edge β̄ → ᾱ. Chaining these along a path gives us the complement path in reverse. Now, suppose S is a sink SCC. For any α ∈ S: There's no edge from α to any vertex outside S (sink property) By the helper fact, there's no edge from any vertex outside S̄ to ᾱ So S̄ has no incoming edges from outside → S̄ is a source SCC ✅ Why Setting Sink = TRUE Works When we set a sink SCC S to TRUE: S has no outgoing edges → no implications to propagate S̄ is set to FALSE. S̄ is a source → all edges from S̄ go outward. But FALSE can imply anything (FALSE → anything is vacuously TRUE), so no contradictions arise. After removing S and S̄, the remaining graph still has valid SCCs, and we repeat. 7. Worked Example f = (x1 ∨ x2) ∧ (x̄1 ∨ x3) ∧ (x̄2 ∨ x̄3) ∧ (x1 ∨ x3) Step 1: Build implication graph Clause Edge 1 Edge 2 (x₁ ∨ x₂) x̄₁ → x₂ x̄₂ → x₁ (x̄₁ ∨ x₃) x₁ → x₃ x̄₃ → x̄₁ (x̄₂ ∨ x̄₃) x₂ → x̄₃ x₃ → x̄₂ (x₁ ∨ x₃) x̄₁ → x₃ x̄₃ → x₁ graph LR nx1["not-x1"] --> x2["x2"] nx2["not-x2"] --> x1["x1"] x1 --> x3["x3"] nx3["not-x3"] --> nx1 x2 --> nx3 x3 --> nx2 nx1 --> x3 nx3 --> x1 Step 2: Find SCCs Let's trace the paths: x̄₁ → x₂ → x̄₃ → x₁ → x₃ → x̄₂ → x₁ (cycle: x₁, x₃, x̄₂, x̄₃ form paths but let's check) x̄₃ → x̄₁ → x₃ (via x̄₁ → x₃) — and x₃ → x̄₂ → x₁ → x₃ (cycle!) SCC analysis: x₁ → x₃ → x̄₂ → x₁: SCC A = {x₁, x₃, x̄₂} x̄₁ → x₂ → x̄₃ → x̄₁: SCC B = {x̄₁, x₂, x̄₃} Note: SCC A and SCC B are complements of each other! ✅ Step 3: Check — no xᵢ and x̄ᵢ in the same SCC. ✅ Satisfiable! Step 4: Assign SCC A = {x₁, x₃, x̄₂} and SCC B = {x̄₁, x₂, x̄₃} Which is the sink? A has edges: x₁ → x₃ (internal), x₃ → x̄₂ (internal), x̄₂ → x₁ (internal). Any outgoing? Check all edges from members of A to non-members... If A is the sink: Set SCC A = TRUE: x₁ = T, x₃ = T, x̄₂ = T → x₂ = F Set SCC B = FALSE: x̄₁ = F, x₂ = F, x̄₃ = F → x₁ = T, x₃ = T ✅ (consistent!) Verify: x₁ = T, x₂ = F, x₃ = T (x₁ ∨ x₂) = (T ∨ F) = T ✅ (x̄₁ ∨ x₃) = (F ∨ T) = T ✅ (x̄₂ ∨ x̄₃) = (T ∨ F) = T ✅ (x₁ ∨ x₃) = (T ∨ T) = T ✅ 8. Unit Clause Simplification (Preprocessing) If a clause has only one literal (a unit clause), we can simplify: Find unit clause with literal α Set α = TRUE Remove all clauses containing α Remove ᾱ from all remaining clauses (may create new unit clauses or empty clauses) If any clause becomes empty → unsatisfiable Repeat This preprocessing reduces the problem before building the implication graph. After removing all unit clauses, every clause has exactly 2 literals. 9. Summary Problem Given 2-CNF formula, find satisfying assignment Key idea Build implication graph, run SCC Satisfiable iff No xᵢ and x̄ᵢ in same SCC Assignment Sink SCC → TRUE, its complement (source) → FALSE Runtime O(n + m) Contrast 3-SAT is NP-complete! 🎯 Exam Tip: The construction of the implication graph is the critical step. Remember: clause (α ∨ β) gives edges ᾱ → β and β̄ → α. Each clause contributes exactly 2 edges. ⚠️ Common Pitfall: Don't confuse the implication graph edges. For (α ∨ β), the implication is "if NOT α, then β" — so the edge goes

title: GR3: Minimum Spanning Tree
text: GR3: Minimum Spanning Tree 📚 Reference: DPV Chapter 5.1 (Greedy Algorithms — MST) MST is our first greedy algorithm that provably works. The key is the Cut Property. 1. Problem Definition Given: An undirected, connected graph G = (V, E) with edge weights w(e) Find: A spanning tree T ⊆ E of minimum total weight: min Σ w(e) for e ∈ T A spanning tree of G is a subset of edges that: Connects all vertices (spans the graph) Has no cycles (it's a tree) Has exactly |V| - 1 edges 2. Tree Properties These three properties are equivalent for a subgraph with n vertices: Property Meaning Connected + n-1 edges → Must be a tree Connected + acyclic → Must have n-1 edges n-1 edges + acyclic → Must be connected Also important: A tree has exactly one path between any pair of vertices Removing any edge disconnects a tree Adding any edge to a tree creates exactly one cycle 💡 Intuition: A tree is the most economical way to keep a graph connected — it uses the minimum number of edges (n-1). An MST does this with minimum total weight. 3. Kruskal's Algorithm Repeatedly add the lightest edge that doesn't create a cycle. Kruskal(G, w): Input: Undirected graph G = (V, E) with weights w(e) Output: MST edge set X 1. Sort E by increasing weight 2. X = ∅ 3. For each edge e = (v, w) in sorted order: if adding e to X doesn't create a cycle: X = X ∪ {e} 4. Return X Cycle Detection with Union-Find How do we efficiently check "does adding (v, w) create a cycle"? Use a Union-Find (disjoint set) data structure: Find(v): Returns the component containing v Union(v, w): Merges the components of v and w Adding edge (v, w) creates a cycle ⟺ Find(v) = Find(w) (already in same component). Kruskal(G, w): // with Union-Find 1. Sort E by increasing weight 2. X = ∅ 3. Initialize Union-Find with each vertex in its own set 4. For each edge e = (v, w) in sorted order: if Find(v) ≠ Find(w): X = X ∪ {e} Union(v, w) 5. Return X Runtime: O(|E| log |V|) Sorting: O(|E| log |E|) = O(|E| log |V|) since |E| ≤ |V|² Union-Find operations: O(|E| · α(|V|)) ≈ O(|E|) (nearly constant per op) Dominated by sorting Worked Example graph LR A ---|1| B A ---|4| C B ---|2| C B ---|3| D C ---|5| D C ---|7| E D ---|6| E Sorted edges: (A,B,1), (B,C,2), (B,D,3), (A,C,4), (C,D,5), (D,E,6), (C,E,7) Step Edge Weight Add? Reason 1 (A,B) 1 ✅ Different components 2 (B,C) 2 ✅ Different components 3 (B,D) 3 ✅ Different components 4 (A,C) 4 ❌ Same component — would create cycle 5 (C,D) 5 ❌ Same component 6 (D,E) 6 ✅ Different components. Done! (4 edges = n-1) MST edges: {(A,B), (B,C), (B,D), (D,E)} with total weight = 1+2+3+6 = 12 graph LR A ---|1| B B ---|2| C B ---|3| D D ---|6| E style A fill:#90EE90 style B fill:#90EE90 style C fill:#90EE90 style D fill:#90EE90 style E fill:#90EE90 4. The Cut Property — Why Kruskal's Works What Is a Cut? Definition: For an undirected graph G = (V, E), a cut is a partition of V into two non-empty sets S and S̄ = V \ S. The cut edges are: cut(S, S̄) = {(v, w) ∈ E : v ∈ S, w ∈ S̄} graph LR subgraph S_set["S"] A B end subgraph Sbar["S-bar"] C D E end A ---|"cut edge"| C B ---|"cut edge"| D A --- B C --- D D --- E The Cut Property Lemma Lemma (Cut Property): Let X ⊆ E be a subset of some MST T. Let S ⊆ V be such that no edge of X crosses the cut (S, S̄). Let e* be a minimum weight edge crossing the cut. Then X ∪ {e*} ⊆ T' for some MST T'. In plain English: A minimum-weight edge across any cut is safe to add to the MST. 💡 Intuition: If I give you any tree T that doesn't include e*, I can swap e* in and some other cut edge out, and the weight won't increase (because e* is the lightest across the cut). So there's always an MST containing e*. Proof of the Cut Property Setup: Fix G, X, T (an MST containing X), and S. e* is a min-weight edge in cut(S, S̄) No edge of X crosses the cut Goal: Show X ∪ {e*} ⊆ T' for some MST T'. Case A: e* ∈ T. Then T' = T. Done. ✅ Case B: e* ∉ T. We construct T'. Add e* to T → creates a unique cycle C (since T is a tree) e* = (a, b) crosses the cut (a ∈ S, b ∈ S̄). The cycle C must cross the cut at least one more time (to get back from S̄ to S). Let e' be another edge of C that crosses the cut. Set T' = T ∪ {e*} \ {e'} Claim 1: T' is a tree. T' has n-1 edges (added one, removed one) T' is connected: any path in T that used e' can detour through the rest of cycle C (which includes e*) Connected + n-1 edges → tree ✅ Claim 2: T' is an MST. w(e*) ≤ w(e') because e* is minimum weight across the cut and e' also crosses the cut w(T') = w(T) + w(e*) - w(e') ≤ w(T) But T is already minimum weight, so w(T') = w(T) ✅ Claim 3: X ∪ {e} ⊆ T'.* X ⊆ T and X has no edges crossing the cut, so e' ∉ X, meaning X ⊆ T' = T \ {e'} ∪ {e*} e* ∈ T' by construction ✅ 🎯 Exam Tip: The proof of the Cut Property is one of the most likely proof questions on the exam. Know the two cases (e* in T or not) and the swap argument. 5. Why Kruskal's Is Correct (via Cut Property) At each step of Kruskal's, when we add edge e = (v, w): v and w are in different components (otherwise we'd skip) Let S = the component containing v, S̄ = everything else No edge of X (edges added so far) crosses this cut (by definition of components) e is the minimum weight edge crossing this cut (because we process edges in sorted order — all lighter edges either don't cross this cut or were already added) By the Cut Property, adding e is safe ✅ By induction, X is always a subset of some MST. When |X| = n-1, X IS that MST. 6. Prim's Algorithm Prim's grows a single tree from a starting vertex, always adding the lightest edge connecting the tree to a non-tree vertex. Prim(G, w): Input: Undirected graph G = (V, E) with weights w(e) Output: MST edge set X X = ∅ S = {any starting vertex} while |X| < |V| - 1: e* = minimum weight edge in cut(S, V\S) X = X ∪ {e*} S = S ∪ {endpoint of e* in V\S} Return X Implementation: Use a min-heap keyed by the lightest edge connecting each non-tree vertex to the tree. Runtime: O(|E| log |V|) with binary heap Correctness: Direct application of the Cut Property at each step (S = tree vertices so far, no edge of X leaves S since the tree is contained in S). Prim's Example Same graph as before, starting from A: graph LR A ---|1| B A ---|4| C B ---|2| C B ---|3| D C ---|5| D C ---|7| E D ---|6| E Step S (tree) Lightest cut edge Add 1 {A} (A,B) w=1 (A,B) 2 {A,B} (B,C) w=2 (B,C) 3 {A,B,C} (B,D) w=3 (B,D) 4 {A,B,C,D} (D,E) w=6 (D,E) Same MST: {(A,B), (B,C), (B,D), (D,E)}, weight = 12 ✅ 7. Kruskal's vs Prim's Kruskal's Prim's Strategy Global: lightest edge overall Local: lightest edge from current tree Grows Forest → single tree Single tree from start Data structure Union-Find Min-heap Runtime O(|E| log |V|) O(|E| log |V|) Better for Sparse graphs Dense graphs Correctness Cut Property (S = component) Cut Property (S = tree so far) Both are just special cases of the generic "greedy + Cut Property" approach. ⚠️ Common Pitfall: MST is for undirected graphs only. The directed analog (minimum spanning arborescence) is a completely different prob

title: GR4: Markov Chains & PageRank
text:
GR4: Markov Chains & PageRank This module connects graph theory, linear algebra, and probability. PageRank uses all three to define the "importance" of a webpage. The key insight: importance = stationary distribution of a random walk. 1. What Is a Markov Chain? A Markov chain is a random process that moves between states over discrete time steps, where the next state depends only on the current state (not the history). Formally: N states: {1, 2, ..., N} Transition matrix P (N × N): P(i, j) = Pr(go from state i to state j) Two key constraints on P: P(i, j) ∈ [0, 1] for all i, j (probabilities) Each row sums to 1: Σⱼ P(i, j) = 1 (from any state, you go somewhere) A matrix with non-negative entries where every row sums to 1 is called a stochastic matrix. Example: The CS 6210 Student graph LR K["Kishore
(lecture)"] -->|0.5| K K -->|0.5| E["Check
Email"] E -->|0.2| K E -->|0.5| S["StarCraft"] E -->|0.3| Z["Sleep"] S -->|0.3| E S -->|0.7| S Z -->|0.7| K Z -->|0.3| Z Note: self-loops are allowed (and common) in Markov chains! Transition matrix: Kishore Email StarCraft Sleep Kishore [ 0.5 0.5 0 0 ] Email [ 0.2 0 0.5 0.3 ] StarCft [ 0 0.3 0.7 0 ] Sleep [ 0.7 0 0 0.3 ] Each row sums to 1. ✅ 2. k-Step Transitions: Powers of P One step: P(i, j) = probability of going from i to j in 1 step. Two steps: P²(i, j) = probability of going from i to j in exactly 2 steps. k steps: Pᵏ(i, j) = probability of going from i to j in exactly k steps. 💡 Intuition: Matrix multiplication naturally sums over all possible intermediate states — exactly what we need for multi-step probabilities. Example: P² for the 6210 chain P² = [ 0.35 0.25 0.25 0.15 ] [ 0.31 0.25 0.35 0.09 ] [ 0.06 0.21 0.64 0.09 ] [ 0.56 0.15 0 0.09 ] (approximate) Verification: P²(2, 1) = Pr(Email → Kishore in 2 steps) = (Email → Kishore → Kishore) + (Email → Sleep → Kishore) = (0.2)(0.5) + (0.3)(0.7) = 0.10 + 0.21 = 0.31 ✅ 3. What Happens for Large k? Something remarkable: as k → ∞, the rows of Pᵏ all converge to the same vector. For our example, P²⁰ ≈ [ 0.292 0.188 0.406 0.104 ] [ 0.292 0.188 0.406 0.104 ] [ 0.292 0.188 0.406 0.104 ] [ 0.292 0.188 0.406 0.104 ] Every row is approximately π = [0.292, 0.188, 0.406, 0.104]. What this means: No matter where you start, after enough time steps, the probability of being in each state converges to π. You'll spend ~29.2% of your time listening to Kishore, ~40.6% playing StarCraft, etc. — regardless of starting state. This π is the stationary distribution. 4. Stationary Distribution Definition: A stationary distribution π is a row vector such that πP = π. In other words: if you're in distribution π and take one step, you stay in π. It's a fixed point of the random walk. Linear Algebra Perspective πP = π means π is a left eigenvector of P with eigenvalue 1. More generally, if μ₀ is your initial distribution (a row vector), then: After 1 step: μ₁ = μ₀ · P After 2 steps: μ₂ = μ₀ · P² After k steps: μₖ = μ₀ · Pᵏ As k → ∞, μₖ → π regardless of μ₀ (under the right conditions). Key Questions Does every Markov chain have a stationary distribution? Is it unique? Do we always converge to it? The answer to all three is yes — if the chain is ergodic. 5. Ergodic Markov Chains A Markov chain is ergodic if it's both: (a) Irreducible (= Strongly Connected) The underlying directed graph is one strongly connected component — you can get from any state to any other state. ⚠️ Pitfall: If there are multiple SCCs, the starting state determines which states you can reach. No unique stationary distribution. graph LR subgraph SCC1 A --> B --> A end subgraph SCC2 C --> D --> C end This is NOT irreducible — starting in SCC1, you never reach SCC2. Fix: Make the graph fully connected (positive probability between every pair). (b) Aperiodic The chain has no periodic structure. A chain is periodic if some states can only be revisited at regular intervals. ⚠️ Pitfall: Bipartite graphs have period 2 — you alternate sides. At odd times you're on one side, even times on the other. graph LR L1 --> R1 L1 --> R2 R1 --> L1 R1 --> L2 R2 --> L2 L2 --> R1 If you start on the left, at every even time you're on the left, every odd time on the right. Starting state matters! Fix: Add self-loops (P(i, i) > 0 for all i). Even a tiny self-loop probability destroys periodicity. The Ergodic Theorem Theorem: If a Markov chain is ergodic (irreducible + aperiodic), then: There exists a unique stationary distribution π For any starting distribution μ₀, μ₀ · Pᵏ → π as k → ∞ Every row of Pᵏ converges to π Sufficient condition (easy to check): If P(i, j) > 0 for ALL i, j (every entry positive), then the chain is ergodic. When Can We Compute π? Case π P is symmetric: P(i,j) = P(j,i) π is uniform: π(i) = 1/N for all i P is reversible: π(i)P(i,j) = π(j)P(j,i) Can solve for π using detailed balance General No closed form — compute via Pᵏ for large k 6. PageRank — Motivation Problem: The web is a directed graph (vertices = pages, edges = hyperlinks). When someone searches for "Markov chains," many pages match. How do you rank them by importance? Idea 1: Count incoming links π(x) = |in(x)| (in-degree) Problem: A link from CNN is worth more than a link from some random page. All links shouldn't count equally. Idea 2: Scale by number of outgoing links π(x) = Σ_{y ∈ in(x)} 1/|out(y)| If page y has 1000 links, each one is worth 1/1000. If it has 5 links, each is worth 1/5. Problem: A link from CNN should be worth more than a link from my kid's homepage, even if they have the same number of outgoing links. The importance of the source matters. Idea 3: Weight by source importance (recursive!) $$\pi(x) = \sum_{y \in in(x)} \frac{\pi(y)}{|out(y)|}$$ A citation from page y is worth π(y)/|out(y)| — proportional to y's importance, divided among its outgoing links. This is a recursive definition — π appears on both sides. Is it well-defined? Is there a unique solution? 💡 Intuition: This is exactly the equation πP = π for a random walk on the webgraph! PageRank = stationary distribution. 7. The Random Surfer Model Imagine a "random surfer" who: Starts at any webpage At each step, follows a random outgoing hyperlink This defines a Markov chain with transition matrix: $$P(y, x) = \begin{cases} 1/|out(y)| & \text{if } y \to x \text{ is a hyperlink} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ The stationary distribution of this chain satisfies exactly the PageRank equation from above! But there are problems: The webgraph has multiple SCCs → not irreducible Parts may be bipartite → periodic Some pages are sink nodes (no outgoing links) → transition undefined 8. Fixing the Problems — The Damping Parameter To ensure ergodicity, we modify the random walk with a damping parameter α ∈ (0, 1): At each step: With probability α: follow a random outgoing hyperlink With probability 1 - α: jump to a random page uniformly from ALL pages This is the random surfer with a "random button." Google reportedly uses α ≈ 0.85. The Modified Transition Matrix For N total webpages: $$P'(y,

title: 📝 Graphs Cheat Sheet — CS 6515
text: 📝 Graphs Cheat Sheet — CS 6515 Algorithms at a Glance Algorithm Input Output Runtime Key Idea BFS G, source s dist[], prev[] O(n+m) Queue; shortest paths (unweighted) Dijkstra's G, s, w(e)>0 dist[], prev[] O((V+E)log V) Min-heap BFS; positive weights only DFS G pre[], post[], ccnum[] O(n+m) Recursion; foundation for everything Topo Sort DAG ordered vertices O(n+m) DFS, sort by decreasing post number SCC Directed G SCC labels O(n+m) DFS on G^R, then DFS on G 2-SAT 2-CNF formula assignment or NO O(n+m) Implication graph + SCC Kruskal's Undirected G, weights MST O(E log V) Sort edges; Union-Find Prim's Undirected G, weights MST O(E log V) Grow tree; min-heap Key Lemmas & Theorems Cycle Detection G has a cycle ⟺ DFS tree has a back edge SCC Source Lemma Vertex with highest post number (in DFS on G^R) is in a source SCC of G^R = sink SCC of G 2-SAT Lemma f is satisfiable ⟺ ∀i, xᵢ and x̄ᵢ are in different SCCs Cut Property (MST) A minimum-weight edge crossing any cut (S, S̄) is in some MST. Proof idea: If MST T doesn't contain e*, add e* (creates cycle), remove another cut edge e'. w(T') ≤ w(T). Ergodic Theorem (Markov Chains) Irreducible + aperiodic ⟹ unique stationary distribution π, and Pᵏ → π regardless of start. Edge Types in DFS (Directed Graphs) Type Direction post(z) vs post(w) Tree Parent → child post(z) > post(w) Back Descendant → ancestor post(z) < post(w) ⚠️ Forward Ancestor → descendant post(z) > post(w) Cross No ancestor relation post(z) > post(w) Only back edges have post(z) < post(w). Everything else: post(z) > post(w). SCC Algorithm — Quick Steps Build G^R (reverse edges) DFS(G^R) → get post numbers Order vertices by decreasing post number DFS(G) in that order → each Explore = one SCC 2-SAT — Quick Steps Build implication graph: clause (α ∨ β) → edges ᾱ→β and β̄→α Run SCC algorithm If any xᵢ and x̄ᵢ in same SCC → unsatisfiable Assign: sink SCC = TRUE, its complement (source) = FALSE, repeat MST — Quick Reminders Kruskal's: Sort edges, add lightest if no cycle (Union-Find) Prim's: Grow one tree, add lightest cut edge (min-heap) Both correct via Cut Property Trees: n-1 edges, connected, acyclic (any 2 ⟹ 3rd) PageRank — Quick Formula $$\pi(x) = \sum_{y \in in(x)} \frac{\pi(y)}{|out(y)|}$$ Equivalent to πP = π (stationary distribution) Damping: P' = αP + (1-α)·(1/N)·J, with α ≈ 0.85 Sink nodes: random jump (α = 0) Compute via power iteration: μ · P'^k → π ⚠️ Common Pitfalls Pitfall Why It's Wrong Using Dijkstra's with negative weights Can give wrong shortest paths; use Bellman-Ford Topological sort on a cyclic graph Impossible — check for back edges first Confusing 2-SAT implication direction (α ∨ β) gives ᾱ→β, NOT α→β Assuming MST is unique Multiple MSTs possible if edges have equal weights (total weight is unique) Assuming webgraph is ergodic It's not — damping parameter is essential Forgetting Markov chains need stochastic matrix Every row must sum to 1 🎯 Exam Tips DFS is the Swiss Army knife — SCCs, topo sort, cycle detection all build on it Cut Property proof — high probability exam question. Know the swap argument. 2-SAT construction — practice converting clauses to implication edges quickly SCC order matters — DFS on G^R first, then G. Not the other way around. Runtimes are all O(n+m) for graph decomposition algorithms (BFS, DFS, SCC, 2-SAT, topo sort). Only MST requires sorting: O(E log V). PageRank = stationary distribution — know the three equivalent views (citation, random walk, eigenvector)
